files.response: This file handles the display of text output in the Codex terminal interface by converting ANSI color codes (formatting that tools use to add colors and styles to text) into a format the UI can render, while also cleaning up tabs to prevent visual glitches in transcript views.

This file is a command-line tool that generates TypeScript code and documentation files describing how the Codex app server communicates with other parts of the system, optionally formatting the output to match code style standards.
This is a maintenance utility that regenerates test data files for the app server communication protocol, optionally including experimental features and formatting the output with a code formatter.

This file provides a system for marking and tracking which features in the Codex API are still experimental and require special permission to use. It allows the server to identify when clients are using unstable features and prevent access unless they explicitly opt into experimental capabilities.

# export.rs

This file generates type definitions and API documentation in TypeScript and JSON formats that describe the communication protocol between clients and servers. It takes the codebase's internal type definitions and exports them as machine-readable schemas and developer-friendly type files, optionally filtering out experimental/preview features for stable releases.

This file defines the message format used for communication between the Codex app server and its clients—it standardizes how requests, responses, notifications, and errors are structured and validated when exchanged over the network.

This file serves as the main entry point for the app server protocol library, organizing and exposing the core communication standards and tools that allow different parts of the Codex system to talk to each other—including protocol definitions, type generation utilities, and test data helpers.

This file defines the communication protocol between the Codex server and its clients (like the VS Code extension). It establishes all the requests, responses, and notifications that can flow in both directions—for example, when a client asks the server to start a thread or execute a command, or when the server asks the client to approve a file change. The file also generates TypeScript definitions and JSON schemas so that clients and the server can understand each other's messages in a consistent format.

This file translates requests between different versions of the app server protocol, allowing older clients (version 1) to work with newer server code (version 2) by automatically converting their command execution parameters to the updated format.

This file organizes the communication protocol used between the app and server—it acts as the main entry point that exposes the different protocol versions and shared message definitions that other parts of the codebase need to use.

This file reconstructs the conversation history (called "turns") from a stored log of events. It takes a sequence of raw system events—like user messages, agent responses, tool calls, and status updates—and organizes them into a readable conversation structure with proper grouping, ordering, and metadata. This allows Codex to display and replay a user's full interaction history, including handling edge cases like out-of-order completions, aborted turns, and conversation rollbacks.

This file defines the request and response formats for Codex's core API operations. It specifies what information clients must send when performing actions like starting conversations, logging in, approving file changes, or sending messages to the AI—and what data the server sends back in response. Essentially, it's the contract that ensures the client application and backend server understand each other when communicating.

# Summary

This file defines the API contract for Codex's version 2 protocol, translating between internal data structures and the external API format that clients use. It establishes what information flows between the app and clients (like the VS Code extension), covering everything from user authentication and configuration to thread management, conversation turns, and real-time progress notifications. The file essentially acts as a gatekeeper, ensuring clients see a consistent, well-documented interface while the internal system can evolve independently.

This file manages the generation and comparison of API schema reference files (in TypeScript and JSON formats). It creates standardized, normalized versions of these schemas that can be reliably compared across different platforms and development environments, ensuring consistency in how the API is documented and validated.

This file is a test that verifies the schema documentation files stored in the repository match what the system would generate fresh, catching cases where schema definitions have changed but the stored documentation wasn't updated. If they don't match, it tells developers to run a command to regenerate the documentation.

This file is a testing tool that lets engineers manually interact with and verify the Codex app-server—a backend service that powers AI-assisted coding features. It provides a command-line interface to send test messages, approve or deny code execution requests, manage conversation threads, and monitor the server's responses in real time. The tool can either spawn the app-server locally or connect to an existing one over the network, making it useful for debugging the server's behavior and validating that features like user messaging, file changes, and command execution work as expected.
This file serves as the entry point for a test tool that connects to and exercises the Codex application server, allowing developers to validate that the server is working correctly.

# Summary

This file handles special event processing for the Codex conversation system, translating internal system events into user-facing notifications and handling user approvals for sensitive operations. It acts as the bridge between the AI engine's internal events and what gets communicated to clients, managing things like approval requests for code changes and command execution, progress updates on various tasks, and conversation state transitions.

# CodexMessageProcessor

This file handles all incoming client requests for Codex's AI assistant features. It processes JSON-RPC messages that control thread (conversation) lifecycle, user interactions, authentication, file searching, and configuration management.

The processor acts as a central hub that translates client API calls into operations on active threads, manages login sessions, handles file discovery, lists available AI models and collaboration modes, and orchestrates event delivery back to clients. It supports both legacy "conversation" APIs and newer "thread/turn" APIs, manages user authentication (API key and ChatGPT), and coordinates real-time event streaming to connected clients listening to specific threads.

This file handles reading and writing Codex configuration settings through an API interface. It translates configuration requests from clients into internal operations and manages settings like approval policies, sandbox security modes, web search capabilities, data residency rules, and network access restrictions.

This file handles receiving responses from external tools that Codex calls during conversations and sends those results back into the conversation thread. If a tool call fails or returns invalid data, it automatically sends an error message to keep the conversation flowing.

This file defines the standard error codes that the server returns to clients when something goes wrong, such as invalid requests, internal failures, or service overload. It ensures consistent error responses across the application so clients know how to interpret and handle different types of problems.

This file handles filtering conversation threads based on where they originated—whether from a user typing in the CLI, VS Code, or from internal Codex processes like sub-agents. It provides logic to determine which source types should be allowed and whether additional filtering is needed, enabling the product to show users only the relevant conversation history based on their chosen filters.

This file powers Codex's fuzzy file search feature, which lets users quickly find files by typing partial names or patterns. It supports two modes: a one-time search that returns all matching files at once, and a persistent search session that continuously updates results as the user refines their query, sending live notifications back to the editor.

This file is the main entry point and orchestrator for Codex's application server, responsible for starting up the system, loading configuration, setting up communication channels, and coordinating message routing between clients and the core processing logic. It handles incoming connections (via stdio or WebSocket), parses configuration with warnings, initializes logging and analytics, and manages two main async tasks—one that processes incoming requests and one that routes outgoing responses to connected clients.

This file is the entry point that starts the Codex app server and configures how it listens for incoming requests. It parses command-line options (like which network endpoint to use), loads configuration settings, and launches the core server logic with the specified transport method (either standard input/output or a network socket).

This file handles incoming requests from the VS Code extension and routes them to the appropriate handlers. It manages the initialization handshake with clients, processes configuration read/write requests, enforces authentication token refresh when needed, and delegates other requests to the core Codex processor for handling code completion and chat features.

This file provides the list of AI models available to users in the app, including details like their names, descriptions, capabilities (such as whether they support reasoning or different input types), and which models are visible in the UI. It fetches this information from a central configuration and formats it for the app to display.

This file handles sending messages from the server back to connected clients. It manages the delivery of responses, errors, notifications, and requests across one or more client connections, and tracks when clients respond to server requests so the system knows when work is complete.

This file tracks the real-time state of individual conversations (threads) in the Codex system, including what clients are connected to them, any errors that occurred, and pending user requests like interrupts or rollbacks. It manages subscriptions so the server knows which clients should receive updates about which conversations, and ensures cleanup when conversations or client connections are closed.

This file handles the network communication layer for the Codex app server, managing how the product accepts incoming requests and sends responses to clients over either standard input/output or WebSocket connections. It parses configuration options for which network protocol to use, establishes connections, routes incoming messages to the message processor, sends outgoing responses back to clients, and intelligently handles network congestion by sending "server overloaded" errors instead of dropping requests.
This file serves as the entry point for the product's integration tests, organizing and running all test scenarios that verify the application server works correctly end-to-end.

This file provides testing utilities for creating fake ChatGPT authentication credentials. It allows test engineers to easily generate mock login data (tokens, user IDs, email addresses, etc.) and write them to a test environment, so they can verify that the product correctly handles user authentication without needing real OpenAI accounts.

This file generates a test configuration file that tells the application how to run in testing mode, including which features to enable, what AI model provider to use (real OpenAI or a mock one), and various behavioral settings like auto-compaction limits and sandbox restrictions.

This file provides a collection of testing utilities and helper functions that allow engineers to set up realistic test scenarios for the Codex application—such as creating mock servers, fake user authentication, simulated model responses, and configuration files. It acts as a shared toolkit so tests throughout the codebase can consistently simulate the system's behavior without needing to write the same setup code repeatedly.

This file provides test infrastructure for communicating with Codex's application server during automated testing. It creates and manages a separate server process that tests can send requests to and receive responses from, simulating real client-server interactions. The file handles the low-level messaging protocol—sending commands like "create a conversation" or "log in," reading back responses, and buffering messages that arrive out of order—so tests can focus on validating the server's behavior without worrying about communication plumbing.

This file provides test utilities that simulate an AI model server for testing purposes. It creates fake servers that respond to requests in predefined ways—either by returning a sequence of different responses one after another, or by repeatedly returning the same assistant message—allowing the product's server code to be tested without needing a real model backend.

This file helps with testing by creating a fake cache of available AI models, so the app doesn't need to make real network requests to fetch the list of models during tests. It takes the built-in model definitions and writes them to a cache file that the product treats as fresh data.

This file provides helper functions that generate realistic test responses for the Codex API, simulating different types of interactions like running shell commands, displaying assistant messages, applying code patches, and requesting user confirmation. It's used in testing to mock what the server would return to clients without needing a real API call.

This file provides testing utilities to create fake session records for Codex. It generates sample conversation files that mimic real user sessions—including metadata like timestamps, git information, and user messages—so developers can test how the system stores and retrieves session data without needing actual user interactions.

This file tests whether conversations can be successfully archived—specifically, it verifies that when a user archives a conversation thread, all its data gets moved from the active conversations folder into an archived storage location where it can be kept for record-keeping without cluttering the current workspace.

This file tests the authentication system for Codex, verifying how users log in and check their authentication status. It runs automated tests covering scenarios like logging in with API keys, checking authentication without credentials, respecting forced login methods, and ensuring sensitive tokens are only returned when requested.

This file tests the end-to-end conversation flow of Codex, verifying that users can start conversations, send messages, receive AI responses, and manage approval policies for running commands. It simulates real interactions by spinning up a mock AI model server and validating that the system correctly handles message exchanges, command approval requests, and parameter changes between turns.

This file contains automated tests that verify the app server correctly reads and parses user configuration settings. It tests two scenarios: that all configuration options (like model choice, sandbox restrictions, and enabled tools) are properly loaded from a config file, and that the system gracefully handles an empty configuration.

This file tests the core conversation workflow—creating a new thread and sending a message through it—to ensure the server properly handles these fundamental interactions with the AI model. It simulates a user creating a conversation, subscribing to updates, sending a message, and verifies that the request reaches the model service correctly.

This file tests the conversation forking feature, which allows users to branch off a new conversation from an existing one while optionally changing settings like the AI model. It verifies that forking creates a proper copy of the conversation history, generates a new conversation ID and file, preserves the original conversation untouched, and returns the correct information back to the user.

This file contains automated tests for the fuzzy file search feature, which lets users search for files by typing partial filenames. The tests verify that the search correctly ranks and returns matching files, handles multiple concurrent search sessions independently, streams live results as the user types, and properly cleans up when searches are canceled or stopped.

This file tests whether the app can successfully interrupt long-running conversations. It simulates a user sending a message that triggers a 10-second command, then verifies that sending an interrupt request stops that command and returns the expected cancellation confirmation.

This file tests whether users can successfully list their past conversations with pagination and filtering, and then resume a previous conversation with the ability to change settings like the AI model. It verifies that conversations can be retrieved by their stored path, by a unique ID, or by providing explicit message history, and that filtering by AI provider correctly narrows results.

This file contains automated tests that verify the login and logout functionality for Codex, including scenarios where users authenticate with API keys or ChatGPT accounts, get logged out, and encounter forced authentication restrictions. It ensures that authentication credentials are properly stored and removed, and that login methods respect admin-configured policies.

This file is a test suite index that organizes and loads all the different test scenarios for the Codex server, covering functionality like user authentication, messaging, file search, thread management, and model configuration.

This file tests whether the app server correctly handles output schemas—templates that specify what format the AI assistant should use when returning answers. It verifies that schemas can be provided with individual user requests and that different requests can use different schemas (or none at all).

This file tests the core messaging functionality of the Codex app—specifically, whether users can send messages in conversations and receive responses from the AI model. It validates several key scenarios: normal message sending, opting into detailed event notifications, handling missing conversations, and ensuring the system correctly switches models when resuming a previous conversation with a different model specified. The tests simulate a mock AI model server and verify that messages flow correctly through the system and produce the expected responses.

This file tests whether the app correctly saves a user's choice of AI model (like switching from GPT-5.1 to GPT-4.1) so that choice persists even after the app restarts. It simulates sending a "set default model" request to the app server and verifies that the new model preference is written to the configuration file.

This test file verifies that the Codex server correctly identifies and reports information about the user's system (operating system, version, architecture, etc.) when asked. It ensures the system properly detects the environment it's running in and responds with accurate device and software details.

This file tests that the product correctly retrieves and returns a user's email address when the app server receives a user information request, validating the integration between authentication storage and the user info feature.

## Summary

This file contains automated tests for the Codex app server's account management features, including login, logout, authentication, and token refresh flows. It verifies that users can log in with API keys or ChatGPT credentials, that their account information is properly stored and retrieved, that authentication tokens can be refreshed when needed, and that the system correctly handles various error cases like unauthorized access or mismatched credentials.

This file contains automated tests that verify the app server correctly enables or disables analytics collection based on configuration settings. Specifically, it ensures that analytics can be turned on or off through a flag, and that the system respects the user's preference when no explicit setting is provided.

This file contains automated tests that verify the app listing feature works correctly. It tests scenarios like retrieving available apps, filtering based on configuration settings, handling pagination, managing cached data, and detecting which apps are currently accessible to users. The tests simulate both the Codex server and a mock upstream service to ensure the app listing logic properly combines data from multiple sources and handles various edge cases like network delays and authentication failures.

This file tests that the server correctly returns a list of collaboration mode presets (like "Plan" and "Default" modes) with their correct settings when requested by the client. It ensures this API contract stays stable and helps catch regressions if the default mode configurations ever change unexpectedly.

This file tests the conversation compression feature in Codex—specifically whether the system can automatically or manually compress long conversation histories to save processing resources. The tests verify that when compression is triggered (either automatically after reaching a message limit or manually by request), the system properly notifies the user that compression has started and completed, and that it correctly rejects invalid compression requests.

This file tests the configuration system that lets users and administrators control how the Codex application behaves. It verifies that settings are correctly read from different configuration sources (user preferences, project-specific settings, and system-wide policies), written back to disk, and properly merged when multiple sources provide conflicting values. The tests ensure version conflicts are caught and that batch updates work correctly.

This file tests that the Codex app server correctly handles multiple WebSocket connections in isolation. It verifies that when two clients connect simultaneously, their requests and responses are properly routed to the correct connection without leaking data between them, and that each connection must be initialized independently before using other features.

This file tests whether Codex properly handles dynamic tools—custom capabilities that users can define and invoke during conversations. It verifies that tool definitions are correctly sent to the AI model, that tool calls are properly routed to clients, and that tool results are accurately fed back into the conversation flow.

This file tests whether the experimental API feature works correctly by verifying that certain advanced features are blocked when clients haven't enabled experimental mode, and allowed when they have. It ensures the product properly enforces capability restrictions so that experimental features remain opt-in and don't accidentally get used by clients that don't support them.

This file tests that when a client requests a list of experimental features from the application server, the server correctly returns metadata about each feature—including its name, current stage (beta, under development, stable, etc.), and whether it's enabled—matching the actual feature configuration in the codebase.

This file tests the initialization process that happens when a client (like the VS Code extension) first connects to the Codex server. It verifies that the server correctly identifies the client, handles configuration options like blocking certain notifications, and rejects invalid client information.

This file organizes and groups the automated tests for version 2 of the API, covering core features like user accounts, conversations (threads), turns, real-time connections, and various configuration settings. It serves as the test suite entry point that ensures the API works correctly across all major product functionality.

This file tests the model listing feature, verifying that the app correctly retrieves and displays available AI models to users. It checks that models are returned with complete information, that pagination works properly when there are many models, that users can optionally see hidden models, and that invalid requests are properly rejected.

This file tests whether the Codex app server correctly handles output schema specifications—rules that define what format the AI assistant's responses should follow. It verifies that when a user provides a schema for a conversation turn, that schema is properly passed along to the AI model, and that different turns can use different schemas independently.

This file tests whether the planning feature correctly extracts and formats plan content from AI responses. It verifies that when the AI includes a plan block in its message, the system properly identifies it, streams it to the user, and marks it as a completed plan item—and conversely, that no plan item is created when the response lacks a plan block.

This file contains automated tests that verify the rate limiting feature works correctly for Codex users. It checks that users must be properly authenticated before accessing their rate limit information, and validates that the system correctly retrieves and reports usage data (like how much of their API quota they've consumed and when it resets).

This file tests the ability of the Codex application to request user input during a conversation. It simulates a complete workflow where a user starts a thread, sends an initial message, the system asks for clarification or confirmation from the user, the user provides answers, and the task completes—verifying that this back-and-forth interaction works correctly end-to-end.

This file tests the code review feature of the Codex application. It verifies that when a user initiates a code review (on a commit, branch, or with custom instructions), the system correctly processes the review, generates findings, and delivers the results back to the user—either inline within the same conversation thread or in a separate detached thread. The tests also ensure the system properly validates input parameters and handles approval workflows when required.

This file tests the skills discovery feature, which allows the system to find and list available skills from different folder locations. It verifies that skills can be loaded from custom user-specified folders, that invalid paths are rejected, that skills are only loaded for the requested folders, and that the system properly caches results until explicitly told to refresh.

This file tests the thread archiving feature, which allows users to move completed conversation threads into an archived storage location. It verifies that archiving only works after a thread has been initialized with at least one user message, and confirms that archived threads are properly moved to a designated archive folder.

This file tests the thread forking feature, which allows users to create a new independent copy of an existing conversation thread while preserving the original. The tests verify that forking creates a properly isolated thread with the same content, doesn't modify the original, and prevents forking threads that haven't been saved yet.

This file is a test suite that validates the thread listing feature—the ability to retrieve and browse a user's conversation history with various filters and sorting options. It tests core functionality like pagination, filtering by AI provider and working directory, sorting by creation or update time, and handling of archived conversations.

This file tests whether the app can correctly retrieve and paginate through a list of active conversation threads. It verifies that when users request their loaded threads, the system returns the right thread identifiers and handles pagination correctly when there are multiple threads to display.

This file tests the "read thread" feature, which allows the app to retrieve conversation history and metadata. It verifies that users can fetch a complete thread (with or without individual conversation turns), that the system correctly handles threads before they're fully set up, and that appropriate errors are returned when requesting detailed conversation data too early.

This file contains automated tests that verify the "resume thread" feature works correctly in Codex. The tests check that users can successfully pick up conversations where they left off, including scenarios where conversations have been saved to disk, are currently running, or need their settings changed (like switching AI models or personalities). It also validates error cases, like attempting to resume a conversation that hasn't been started yet or when required system components fail to initialize.

This is a test file that verifies the "rollback" feature works correctly—specifically, that users can undo recent conversation turns and have those changes persist when they resume the conversation later. It simulates creating a thread, running two turns of conversation, rolling back to remove the second turn, and then confirming the history was properly pruned.

This file tests the thread creation feature in Codex. It verifies that when a user starts a new conversation thread, the system correctly creates it with the right settings, sends back a confirmation, respects project-specific configuration, handles temporary threads without saving them to disk, and properly reports errors if required integrations fail to load.

This file tests the unarchive feature for conversation threads, verifying that when a user unarchives a thread, it moves the conversation data back from long-term storage into the active sessions directory and updates its timestamp to reflect the action.

This file tests whether the system can successfully stop a conversation turn that's actively running. It simulates starting a long-running command, then sends an interrupt request to cancel it, and verifies that the turn properly ends with an "interrupted" status.

This file contains integration tests that verify how the "turn start" feature works in the Codex application—specifically, how the system handles user input and processes it through the AI model within a conversation thread.

The tests validate key product behaviors: accepting user text and images as input, supporting model and personality customization on a per-turn basis, managing approval workflows for code execution and file changes (so users can review before Codex makes changes), handling sandbox and working directory settings, preserving user preferences like "never ask for approval" across turns, and ensuring proper status notifications are sent back to the client. Together, these tests ensure that users can start new conversation turns with customized settings, receive appropriate approval requests when configured, and see real-time updates as the AI processes their requests.

This file tests the "turn steering" feature, which allows users to provide new input or redirect an ongoing conversation turn while it's actively running. The tests verify that steering requests are rejected if no turn is in progress, and that steering correctly identifies and returns the active turn being modified.

This file parses commands that invoke the `apply_patch` tool and extracts patch data from them. It handles two invocation patterns: direct calls like `apply_patch <patch>` and shell scripts (bash, PowerShell, cmd) that embed patches in heredoc syntax. It validates the command structure to ensure patches are invoked correctly and resolves file paths relative to the working directory before applying changes.

This file implements a patch application system that parses and applies code changes to files on disk. It reads patch instructions (which specify files to add, delete, or modify), validates them against the actual file contents, applies the changes to the filesystem, and reports which files were affected. The system is designed to work with AI-generated patches, particularly from GPT-4, and ensures changes are applied accurately before execution.

This file serves as the entry point for a command-line tool that applies patches to code. It delegates to the core patching logic located elsewhere in the codebase, allowing users to run patch operations from the terminal.

This file parses patch instructions that tell Codex how to modify files. It reads a specially-formatted patch text (marked with `*** Begin Patch` and `*** End Patch`), breaks it into individual operations like "add this file," "delete that file," or "change these specific lines," and validates that the instructions are well-formed so they can be applied to the codebase.

This file finds where a specific sequence of lines appears in a file so that code patches can be applied correctly. It's flexible about matching—it first tries an exact match, then ignores trailing whitespace, then ignores all whitespace, and finally converts fancy Unicode characters (like curly quotes and em-dashes) to their plain ASCII equivalents to handle files that may have been edited in word processors or other tools. This helps patches apply successfully even when source files have minor formatting differences.

This file enables the patch-application tool to run as a standalone command-line program, accepting patch instructions either as a command argument or through standard input, then executing the patch and reporting success or failure through exit codes.

This file serves as the entry point for running all integration tests in the codebase by aggregating test modules that are organized in a separate folder, ensuring the product's functionality works correctly end-to-end.

This file contains automated tests that verify the patch-applying tool works correctly—specifically, that it can successfully add new files and update existing ones when receiving patch instructions either as command-line arguments or through standard input.

This file organizes the test suite for the patch-application tool by grouping together different categories of tests—command-line interface tests, real-world usage scenarios, and platform-specific tool tests—so they can be run together to verify the feature works correctly.

This file runs automated tests to verify that the patch-applying tool works correctly across different scenarios. It does this by setting up test cases with sample files, running the patch tool on them, and checking that the final result matches what's expected—helping catch bugs before the feature reaches users.

This file contains automated tests that verify the patch-applying tool works correctly. It tests whether the tool can properly create, modify, delete, and move files according to patch instructions, and that it correctly rejects invalid or impossible operations with appropriate error messages.

This file implements a system that allows Codex to deploy as a single executable while appearing as multiple command-line tools. When users invoke Codex through different names (like `apply_patch`), it automatically routes to the correct functionality, and it ensures helper tools are available on the system PATH without requiring separate installation. It also handles loading user configuration from a dotenv file and manages temporary directories that hold these helper tools.

This file provides a utility that allows long-running operations in Codex to be interrupted or cancelled on demand. It wraps any operation with a cancellation token, so if a cancellation signal is received (from a user action or system request), the operation stops immediately and returns an error instead of waiting for completion.

This file implements a communication bridge between the Codex application and its backend servers. It handles making requests to fetch and create coding tasks, check usage limits and credits, retrieve configuration files, and view alternative solution attempts—all while managing authentication headers and supporting connections to different server endpoints (Codex or ChatGPT's backend).

This file serves as the public interface for a backend communication library, exposing the main client for connecting to a backend service along with the data types needed to send and receive information about code tasks, configuration, and task lists.

This file defines the data structures that represent task execution details for Codex code generation tasks, including how to extract and present key information like code changes, assistant messages, user prompts, and errors. It acts as a bridge between the backend API responses and the frontend or internal systems that need to display or process information about what happened when Codex ran a coding task.

This file implements the "apply" command that takes code changes generated by a Codex agent and patches them into a user's local codebase. It retrieves the latest diff from a completed task and uses Git to apply those changes to the user's files, reporting success or any conflicts that occurred.

This file handles communication with OpenAI's ChatGPT backend by making authenticated requests on behalf of the user—it retrieves the user's login credentials, constructs properly formatted API calls, and returns the results so other parts of the product can access ChatGPT data.

This file manages the ChatGPT authentication token used by the application. It provides functions to store and retrieve the user's token in memory, and includes logic to load the token from the system's authentication configuration file when the application starts up.

This file manages the discovery and listing of integrations (called "connectors") that users can add to ChatGPT. It fetches available integrations from OpenAI's directory and workspace sources, caches the results to avoid repeated requests, and filters out any integrations that shouldn't be shown to users. It also merges integrations from multiple sources and marks which ones the user has already enabled.

This file handles retrieving task information from an external service (likely an internal task management system). It defines the expected format of the response data and provides a function that fetches a specific task by its ID, extracting relevant details like code changes or pull request diffs that the AI assistant needs to work with.

This file serves as the main entry point for the ChatGPT integration module, organizing and exposing the key components that other parts of the codebase need to interact with the ChatGPT service—including command execution, client communication, token management, and task retrieval.

This file serves as the entry point for the product's integration tests, organizing and running all test scenarios that verify the system works correctly end-to-end. It acts as a central hub that pulls in individual test modules from a separate suite folder, allowing the team to validate that features work together as expected.

This file tests whether the code-generation feature can correctly apply changes to a user's codebase. It verifies two scenarios: that the system successfully creates new files with the right content, and that it properly handles situations where changes conflict with existing code (by marking the conflicts rather than silently overwriting).

This file organizes the product's end-to-end tests by bringing together test modules in one place, making it easier to run and manage the full test suite that validates how the system works in real-world scenarios.

This file handles the command-line interface for launching Codex as a desktop application on Mac. It lets users specify which folder to open in Codex and optionally override where the application installer is downloaded from.

This file is the command-line interface for testing Codex in different security sandbox environments (macOS Seatbelt, Linux Landlock, and Windows sandboxes). It loads configuration, sets up the appropriate sandbox, runs a user-supplied command inside it, and optionally logs any security policy violations that occurred.

This file tracks all child processes spawned by a given process, including grandchildren and deeper descendants. It monitors process creation and termination events on macOS to maintain an up-to-date list of every process in the family tree, which helps the sandbox environment know which processes to manage or clean up.

This file monitors sandbox security restrictions on macOS during code execution in the debug environment. It captures and logs any attempts by running processes to access capabilities they're not allowed to use, then reports these violations back to the developer so they can understand what permissions their code needs.

This file handles installing and launching the Codex Desktop application on Mac. It checks if the app is already installed on the user's computer, and if not, it downloads the installer, sets it up in the Applications folder, and then opens it to the user's workspace.

This file handles launching or installing a desktop application on macOS, routing the request to the appropriate platform-specific code that either opens an existing app or downloads and installs a new one.

This file ensures the CLI tool properly exits with the correct status code when running on different operating systems (Unix/Linux and Windows), preserving any error signals from the system so the calling process knows whether the command succeeded or failed.

This file defines the command-line interface options for running code in isolated sandboxed environments across different operating systems (macOS, Linux, and Windows). It allows users to specify which sandbox technology to use, whether to run in automatic mode with minimal friction, and optionally capture security violation attempts on macOS.

This file handles user authentication for the Codex command-line tool, supporting multiple login methods: ChatGPT browser-based login, API key login, and device code login. It provides commands to log in, check login status, and log out, while respecting configuration rules that may restrict which login methods are available.

This file is the main entry point for the Codex command-line tool. # Summary

This file implements the **core orchestration engine for the Codex AI assistant**. It manages conversation sessions, coordinates model interactions, handles user input, processes tool calls, and maintains conversation history.

## Key Responsibilities

**Session Management**: The `Codex` struct serves as the high-level public interface—clients submit operations through it and receive events back. The `Session` struct manages the actual conversation state, including history, configuration, active turns, and communication with backend services (models, tools, authentication).

**Turn Processing**: The `run_turn` function executes a single conversation turn by repeatedly calling the model, collecting its responses, executing requested tools, and feeding results back to the model until it produces a final message rather than requesting more tool calls.

**Tool Integration**: Builds and routes tool calls to appropriate handlers (shell commands, file operations, web search, MCP servers, etc.), managing approval workflows and sandbox policies along the way.

**History & Context Management**: Tracks conversation history, applies automatic compaction when reaching token limits, handles session resumption and forking, and manages initial context seeding with permissions and environment information.

**Configuration & Personalization**: Applies per-turn configuration overrides (model, reasoning effort, approvals, sandbox policies, personality preferences), manages MCP server connections, and handles feature flags.

**Event Streaming**: Provides a real-time event channel to clients showing model output deltas, plan segments, token usage, and system notifications as they happen.

This file manages sub-agents that run in parallel to help with code review and other tasks. It creates isolated Codex instances that can be started in two modes—interactive (ongoing back-and-forth) or one-shot (single request with automatic shutdown)—and handles the communication between the sub-agent and its parent by filtering out approval requests that need to bubble up to the user for decision-making, while passing through other events and incoming commands to the sub-agent.

This file represents a single conversation thread in Codex, serving as the main interface through which the product sends and receives messages with the AI agent. It wraps the underlying AI engine and exposes operations like submitting user requests, receiving AI responses, checking the agent's current status, and retrieving thread configuration such as which AI model is being used and what safety policies are in effect.

This file normalizes how commands are represented so that Codex can recognize when the same command is being run in different ways and apply saved approval decisions consistently. For example, it treats `/bin/bash -lc "cargo test"` and `bash -lc "cargo test"` as equivalent, while also handling complex shell scripts and PowerShell commands as special cases.

This file handles **conversation compaction** — the process of summarizing long chat histories to fit within the model's memory limits so Codex can continue working effectively on extended conversations.

When a conversation gets too long, this code automatically summarizes older messages (asking the AI model to create a condensed version), then replaces the lengthy history with that summary plus recent user messages. It manages retries if the compaction request fails, handles edge cases like oversized messages, and ensures important context (like project instructions and environment details) stays fresh and isn't lost in the compression process.

This file handles conversation history compression—when a conversation gets too long for the AI model to process, this code summarizes or condenses the old messages so the model can still understand the context without hitting memory limits. It communicates the compression progress back to the user and ensures important information (like undo snapshots) isn't lost during the process.
