It performs three key maintenance tasks: it automatically fills in missing responses for function calls that didn't complete (marking them as "aborted"), it removes orphaned responses that don't have matching function calls, and it strips out images from messages when the model doesn't support image input. Together, these functions prevent invalid or malformed conversation states from reaching the model.

This file manages custom prompts that users can create and use in Codex. It discovers prompt files (stored as Markdown documents) from a designated directory, reads their metadata like descriptions and usage hints, and makes them available for the product to display and execute. It also handles parsing optional metadata headers from prompt files so the UI can show helpful information about each prompt.

This file sets up how Codex identifies itself when communicating with services—it manages the product's "User-Agent" and "originator" headers that get sent with every API request. It allows different clients (like VS Code or the CLI) to be distinguished from each other, supports data residency requirements (like keeping requests in the US), and provides a convenient way to create pre-configured HTTP clients that automatically include these identification details.

This file detects the computing environment where Codex is running—checking whether it's in Windows Subsystem for Linux, running over SSH, in a CI/CD system, or without a graphical display. The product uses these detections to adapt its behavior, such as avoiding browser-based login flows when there's no GUI available.

This file captures and communicates the user's execution environment (working directory, shell type, and network access rules) to the AI model in a format it can understand. It tracks how the environment changes between conversation turns and formats this information as structured data that gets sent to the model to provide context for code generation.

This file defines how Codex handles and communicates errors to users. It establishes a comprehensive error system that categorizes different failure modes—like network issues, rate limits, sandbox execution failures, and authentication problems—then formats them into user-friendly messages appropriate for each situation, such as upgrade prompts for free-tier users who hit usage limits or specific recovery instructions for expired credentials.

This file translates conversation data from the model's internal format into the structured format used throughout the Codex product. It takes raw message responses and converts them into standardized turn items that represent user messages, assistant replies, reasoning steps, and web search actions—filtering out internal metadata like instructions and image markup tags along the way.

This file handles executing shell commands safely within sandboxed environments. It manages running user-provided commands (like when a code assistant executes code), capturing their output, enforcing timeouts, and detecting when the sandbox has blocked the command from doing something. The file also intelligently truncates large output streams and can stream live command output back to clients as it's generated.

This file controls which environment variables (like PATH, API keys, etc.) are passed to code that Codex executes, based on a configurable security policy. It filters out sensitive variables by default (those containing "KEY", "SECRET", or "TOKEN"), allows customization through allowlists and blocklists, and automatically injects a thread identifier so executed code can identify which Codex session it belongs to.

This file manages command execution policies—a system that determines whether commands can run, require user approval, or are forbidden. It loads policy rules from configuration files, evaluates commands against those rules, and allows users to approve and save new safe commands for future use.

This file manages Codex's feature flags—a centralized control system that lets users turn experimental and new capabilities on or off. It defines which features are available (like web search, sandbox environments, and multi-agent collaboration), tracks their maturity level (stable, experimental, or under development), sets their defaults, and handles reading feature settings from user configuration files. The file also detects when users enable unstable features and warns them that these may behave unpredictably.

This file manages backward compatibility for old feature toggle names by mapping them to their current equivalents. When users or systems reference outdated setting names (like "connectors" or "enable_experimental_windows_sandbox"), this code translates them to the modern feature names the product now uses, while logging a notice to encourage migration away from the legacy names.

This file monitors folders where skills are stored and notifies other parts of the system when skill files are created, modified, or deleted. It batches up rapid file changes together and limits how often it sends notifications to avoid overwhelming the system with updates.

This file defines configuration options that control how the system behaves during testing—specifically, it allows tests to use pre-recorded responses instead of making real network requests to the API.

This file defines the different types of errors that can occur when the system attempts to execute a function or tool call—such as when communicating results back to the model, when a local command is missing required identifiers, or when a critical failure happens—so that these errors can be properly reported and handled throughout the product.

# Summary

This file provides Git repository information to the Codex product by querying Git commands from the file system. It extracts details like the current commit hash, branch name, repository URL, recent commit history, and uncommitted changes—enabling Codex to understand the state of a user's codebase and compare it against remote versions.

This file serves as an organizational hub that exposes instructions-related functionality to the rest of the codebase, specifically making user instruction definitions and their associated formatting markers available for use throughout the system.

This file defines how the system recognizes and formats two types of instruction messages: general user instructions (from documentation files) and skill-specific instructions (from individual skill definitions). It enables the AI agent to identify these special instruction messages in conversations and convert them into a standardized format that the rest of the system can process.

This file manages running user commands in a restricted Linux sandbox environment. It translates Codex's internal security policies into command-line options for a Linux sandbox helper tool, which then uses system-level isolation techniques to safely execute code while controlling what files and network resources the code can access.

This file is the main entry point for Codex Core, the foundational library that powers the Codex AI assistant. It organizes and exposes all the internal components the product needs—such as authentication, model management, sandboxing, conversation history, shell command execution, security policies, and AI model connections—so that other parts of the system can use them consistently. Think of it as the "blueprint" that tells the rest of the product what building blocks are available and how to access them.

This file manages authentication status for MCP servers that Codex connects to. It checks whether each server supports OAuth login or other authentication methods, and gathers the current authentication state for all configured servers so the system knows whether users need to log in to access them.

This file manages the integration of external tools and services into Codex through a protocol called MCP (Model Context Protocol). It discovers what tools and resources are available from various connected services, handles user authentication with those services, and makes that information accessible to the system. The file also includes special logic for connecting to OpenAI's built-in apps and connectors service.

This file handles automatically detecting and installing external server dependencies that skills require to function. When a user selects skills that need additional servers, the code identifies what's missing, asks the user for permission to install them (unless in full-access mode), and then installs and authenticates them into the user's configuration.

# MCP Connection Manager

This file manages connections to external Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers and makes their tools available to the AI model. It handles server startup, tool discovery, tool filtering, caching, and execution—essentially acting as a hub that aggregates capabilities from multiple MCP servers into a unified interface the model can use.

This file manages when and how external tools (called "apps") can be executed within a conversation. It acts as a gatekeeper that may ask users for permission before running certain tools—particularly those that could modify data or contact external services—and then executes the tool and reports back the results. It also tracks which apps have been used for analytics purposes.

This file manages Codex's memory system, which learns from user sessions and consolidates that learning over time. It organizes the process into two phases: first extracting raw memories from recent user interactions, then consolidating those memories into a refined summary that helps the model perform better on future tasks. The file serves as the control center, defining configuration like which AI model to use, how many concurrent jobs to allow, and how long before retrying failed operations.

This file implements the first stage of Codex's memory system, which extracts and summarizes information from past conversations. It identifies relevant conversation threads, sends them to an AI model for analysis, and stores the resulting summaries and memory notes for future reference. The file orchestrates this process in parallel, tracking success rates and token usage for monitoring purposes.

This file orchestrates the second phase of Codex's memory system, which consolidates and summarizes past interactions. It coordinates the workflow: claiming a consolidation job from the database, retrieving recent memories, syncing them to local storage, spawning an AI agent to summarize them, monitoring the agent's progress with periodic health checks, and marking the job as complete or failed based on the outcome.

This file generates the text prompts that Codex sends to AI models during different stages of its memory management process. It formats information about code changes and existing memories into structured prompts, and automatically shortens large amounts of text to fit within the model's input limits while preserving the most important parts from the beginning and end.

This file sets up the memory initialization process when a user starts a new conversation session with Codex. It runs two sequential setup phases to prepare the memory system, but skips this setup for temporary sessions, disabled memory features, or sub-sessions—and warns if the required database isn't available.

This file manages how Codex stores and organizes its memory files on disk. It takes memory data from the system's database and writes it out as readable documentation files, keeping old summaries cleaned up and synced with current state.

This file contains automated tests for the memory system that stores and manages conversation summaries and raw data. It verifies that memories are properly organized on disk, old memories are cleaned up when newer ones arrive, filename rules are enforced correctly, and the consolidation process that periodically rebuilds memory artifacts works as expected across various scenarios including error conditions.

This file handles loading and processing trace files—records of conversations or interactions—and converts them into memory summaries that the AI can reference later. It reads trace files in various formats, extracts relevant conversation items, filters out unwanted data, and sends them to the AI model to generate concise summaries that can be stored as reusable context.

This file identifies and tracks which apps and skills the user has explicitly mentioned or referenced in their messages. It extracts mentions from user input (both direct mentions and references embedded in text), deduplicates them, and builds lookup tables to help the system understand what tools the user wants to use.

This file manages a persistent log of all messages exchanged in conversations. It saves each message to a file (`~/.codex/history.jsonl`) so users can review past interactions, while automatically deleting old messages when the log grows too large to respect storage limits and privacy concerns.

This file manages the configuration for different AI model providers that Codex can connect to—like OpenAI, Azure, Ollama, and LM Studio. It defines what information is needed to set up each provider (such as the API endpoint, authentication credentials, and connection settings), allows users to customize providers in their config file, and provides sensible defaults so the product works out-of-the-box without configuration.

This file manages a local disk cache of AI models available through the Codex product, storing and retrieving them to avoid repeatedly fetching the same list from the server. It checks whether a cached copy is still valid based on age and version, saves fresh model lists to disk for future use, and helps the system decide when to fetch updated information versus reusing what's already stored locally.

This file defines the built-in collaboration mode presets that users can choose from when working with Codex—currently a "Plan" mode and a "Default" mode. Each preset comes with specific instructions and reasoning settings that guide how the AI assistant behaves in that mode.

# ModelsManager

This file manages the discovery and caching of AI models available to users. It fetches model information from the server when authenticated, caches that data locally to reduce network requests, and presents a sorted, filtered list of models that the user can select from—handling different scenarios like offline access, cache expiration, and authentication state changes.

This file organizes the code that manages different AI models available in Codex, including their settings, caching, and preset configurations, and provides a utility to track the product's version number.

This file manages the configuration and metadata for different AI models that power Codex. It allows the system to override model settings based on configuration (like context window size and instruction prompts), create fallback descriptions for unknown models, and define personality variants so users can choose whether they want a supportive or pragmatic coding assistant.

This file defines the catalog of AI models available to users in Codex, including their names, descriptions, capabilities, and configuration options. It specifies which models appear in the UI, which are actively supported, and how to guide users when upgrading to newer model versions.

This file handles loading and monitoring the network proxy configuration for Codex. It reads network settings from various configuration sources (system, user, and project files), validates them against security constraints set by administrators, and watches for changes so the system can automatically reload if configuration files are modified.

This file sets up the observability and monitoring system for the Codex application. It translates the app's configuration settings into instructions for where and how to send performance data, traces, and metrics (such as to external monitoring services), and includes a filter to ensure only Codex-owned monitoring events are exported.

This file handles low-level file system path operations for Codex, ensuring files are read and written correctly across different environments. It safely resolves shortcut links (symlinks) by following them to their final destination while detecting infinite loops, normalizes file paths for case-sensitivity differences when running on Windows Subsystem for Linux, and provides atomic file writing that prevents corruption if the process is interrupted mid-write.

This file handles a one-time migration that automatically sets a user's conversation personality to "Pragmatic" when upgrading Codex, but only if they have existing conversation history and haven't already chosen a personality preference. It tracks whether the migration has run to avoid repeating it.

This file enables Codex to automatically discover and load project-level documentation to guide the AI model's behavior. It searches for `AGENTS.md` files starting from the current directory and walking up to the Git repository root, then combines them with any configured system instructions, JavaScript runtime guidance, and available skills into a single instruction set that shapes how the AI operates within that project.

This file recognizes and processes special `<proposed_plan>` blocks in AI-generated text. It identifies where these plan sections begin and end, extracts just the plan content when needed, or removes the plan markers to leave only surrounding text—enabling the product to handle plan-mode responses from Codex in a structured way.

This file formats code review results into readable text for users. It takes review findings (issues, comments, and their locations in the code) and converts them into a clean, human-readable message that can be displayed in the product's interface, optionally showing checkboxes to indicate which findings have been selected.

This file prepares code review requests by generating the right instructions and user-friendly descriptions based on what the user wants reviewed—whether it's uncommitted changes, a specific branch comparison, a particular commit, or custom instructions they provide.

This file handles error messages that users see when Codex has trouble starting up or accessing its session storage. It translates technical file system problems into helpful, actionable guidance—for example, telling users how to fix permission issues or what to do if session files are corrupted.

This file manages listing and pagination of recorded conversation threads from disk storage. It scans through directories of session files, extracts metadata like timestamps and git information, and returns paginated results sorted by creation or modification date—allowing the UI to display a browsable history of conversations while handling large numbers of files efficiently.

This file manages the indexing and storage of user sessions in Codex. It reads session files from disk, extracts metadata like timestamps, git information, and tool usage, and stores this information in a database so that past sessions can be queried and displayed to users. It also handles a backfill process that periodically scans for any new or missed session files and adds them to the database.

This file organizes the system for managing user coding sessions—storing them, finding them, and tracking their metadata on disk. It defines where sessions are saved, which interfaces (like the CLI or VS Code) can create sessions, and exposes the main tools other parts of the product use to record sessions and look them up by ID or name.

This file defines which conversation events and responses should be saved to disk when Codex runs. It categorizes different types of events into two persistence levels—Limited (core events needed to resume sessions) and Extended (additional diagnostic events)—allowing the system to record conversations at different levels of detail depending on what's needed.

# RolloutRecorder

This file saves and retrieves conversation session recordings (called "rollouts") so users can replay, inspect, and resume their Codex sessions. It writes each session's events to a timestamped file on disk, manages listing and browsing past sessions, and coordinates between in-memory buffering and persistent storage.

This file maintains a searchable index of conversation threads (sessions) so users can look up their conversations by name or ID and retrieve the most recent version if a thread has been renamed. It stores thread metadata in an append-only log file and provides functions to add new entries, look up thread names by ID, look up thread IDs by name, and batch-retrieve multiple thread names.

This file contains automated tests for Codex's conversation history management system. Specifically, it verifies that the product correctly lists, retrieves, and filters user conversations (called "threads") that are stored in session files, including proper handling of pagination, sorting, file timestamps, and filtering by conversation source and AI model provider. The tests also ensure that the system can recover gracefully when stored metadata becomes outdated by falling back to re-scanning the file system.

This file handles trimming conversation history to specific points in a user's interaction with Codex. It identifies where each user message appears in a conversation thread and can cut the history before a particular user message—useful for controlling context length or replaying conversations from certain checkpoints. It also accounts for situations where previous turns were rolled back and removed from the effective history.

This file decides whether code patches are safe to apply automatically or need user approval. It analyzes proposed file changes to determine if they stay within allowed project boundaries, what type of security sandbox (if any) should be used, and whether to auto-approve the patch, ask the user, or reject it entirely.

This file determines which security sandbox mode the code execution environment should use based on the system's configuration and security policy, then labels it with a tag for tracking and reporting purposes.

This file manages the security sandboxing layer for code execution. It takes a generic command specification and transforms it into a platform-specific execution request by wrapping it with the appropriate sandbox protections for the operating system (macOS Seatbelt, Linux Seccomp, or Windows restrictions), while respecting the sandbox policy and permissions defined for that execution.

# seatbelt.rs

This file implements macOS sandbox security policies that restrict what code can do when it runs. It generates detailed security rules that control file access, network connections, and system permissions for executed commands, then enforces those rules using macOS's built-in sandboxing system.

This file manages macOS security permissions for the Codex product when it runs on Apple computers. It translates high-level permission requests (like "allow reading preferences" or "allow controlling other apps") into low-level security rules that macOS enforces to protect user data and system access.

This file defines how the system recognizes special configuration messages that provide context to the AI model across multiple conversation turns—such as environment setup or information about failed attempts—without treating them as actual user inputs that would create new conversation turns.

This file detects and configures the user's command-line shell (like Bash, Zsh, or PowerShell) so Codex can execute commands in the appropriate environment. It identifies which shell is installed, locates its file path, and provides the correct syntax for running commands across different operating systems and shell types.

This file identifies which command-line shell environment the user is running (like Bash, Zsh, PowerShell, etc.) by examining the shell's file path, which allows the product to tailor its behavior and output format to match the user's specific shell.

This file captures and stores snapshots of the user's shell environment (functions, aliases, variables, settings) so that Codex can recreate that environment when needed for code generation or execution tasks. It handles creating shell-specific snapshot files, validating them, and cleaning up old snapshots to manage disk space.

This file handles gathering secret configuration values that certain skills need to run. It checks if these values already exist in the session cache or the system environment, and if they're missing, it prompts the user through the UI to provide them—storing the answers in memory for the duration of the conversation.

This file handles the injection of Skills—reusable tools or extensions—into the system based on what users explicitly mention in their requests. It reads skill files from disk, parses user input to find skill references (like `$skill-name` or linked paths), and deduplicates them while respecting user intent and system constraints like disabled skills. It also tracks analytics on which skills were successfully loaded and which failed.

# Skill Loader

This file discovers and loads "skills" — reusable modules or extensions — from various locations on a user's system (project repositories, home directory, system folders) and reads their metadata. It parses skill definition files, validates their content against size limits, and organizes them by scope (repo-level, user-level, system, or admin) so the product can make skills available to users in the right context.

This file manages the discovery and loading of skills—reusable capabilities or extensions—that are available to Codex based on the user's working directory and configuration. It maintains a cache to avoid repeatedly scanning for skills in the same location, and supports loading skills from multiple sources including system defaults, user configuration, and custom directories provided at runtime.

This file organizes and exposes the skills system, which handles loading, managing, and injecting capabilities (called "skills") that Codex can use to accomplish tasks. It acts as the main entry point for other parts of the product to access skill-related functionality like discovering available skills, determining which ones to use for a given task, and preparing them for execution.

This file defines the data structure for skills in Codex—the reusable capabilities or tools that the system can execute. It stores metadata about each skill (name, description, icons, dependencies) and tracks which skills are loaded, disabled, or allowed to run automatically without explicit user request.

This file manages downloading and installing skills from a remote server. It fetches a list of available skills, downloads individual skill packages as compressed files, extracts them safely to the user's local system, and validates that the downloaded content is legitimate before installation.

This file generates the documentation that teaches the AI assistant how to discover and use "skills"—reusable instruction sets stored as files in the codebase. It formats a readable guide that lists available skills, explains when to use them, and provides step-by-step rules for safely accessing and executing skill instructions without overloading the system with unnecessary file reads.

This file manages the built-in skills that come with Codex. It installs these pre-packaged skills to disk when the application starts, checks whether they've already been installed to avoid redundant work, and detects when the built-in skills have been updated so it can refresh them.

This file handles launching subprocesses that Codex spawns when executing user commands, with controls for security sandboxing and network access. It sets up environment variables and process isolation rules to ensure spawned processes respect the configured security policies—for example, blocking network access or disconnecting from the terminal depending on the task requirements.

This file organizes the core state management pieces of the system by bundling together session tracking, conversation turn management, and service coordination—making these essential components available to the rest of the application.

This file defines the core collection of services and managers that run during a user session in Codex, bundling together everything needed to execute code, manage models, handle authentication, track analytics, and coordinate with external tools and extensions.

This file manages the persistent information that Codex keeps track of during a user's session, such as conversation history, token usage, rate limits, and which external tools or services are currently active. It provides a central place to store and update session-level state that persists across multiple interactions within the same session.

This file manages the state and lifecycle of a single "turn" in a conversation—tracking what tasks are currently running, what approvals or user inputs are waiting to be processed, and coordinating cancellation when needed. It essentially serves as the central hub for organizing all the work happening during one exchange with the user.

This file manages the connection between Codex and an optional SQLite database that stores conversation thread information. It provides functions to initialize the database, look up conversations, list threads with filtering and sorting, and keep the database in sync with conversation files on disk—allowing the system to quickly find and retrieve conversation history without scanning the entire file system every time.

This file handles what happens when the AI model finishes generating each piece of output—whether that's text, a tool request, or reasoning. It records the output to conversation history, queues up any tool calls for execution, and determines if the AI needs to respond further, ensuring the conversation transcript stays consistent even if a turn gets cancelled.

This file enables the system to recognize and separate specially-marked content blocks (like `<proposed_plan>` tags) from regular text as it streams in piece by piece. It's designed to work with content that arrives gradually, allowing clients to identify and handle tagged sections separately—for example, streaming a plan's content to the user interface independently from other response text.

This file implements a task that compresses the conversation history to keep sessions manageable and performant—it decides whether to run this compression locally on the user's machine or remotely on Codex's servers, and tracks how often each approach is used.

This file handles creating automatic Git snapshots of a repository's state in the background, which allows users to undo changes later. It creates a snapshot, monitors how long the process takes, and alerts users if large untracked files are slowing things down or being excluded from the snapshot.

This file manages the execution of different types of AI conversation tasks (like regular chat, code reviews, and undo operations) as background work within a session. It provides a framework for launching, tracking, and canceling these tasks, handling cleanup when they complete or get interrupted by the user, and ensuring the session stays synchronized with task progress.

This file handles the standard request processing flow for Codex—it manages how user input gets turned into code completions. It includes an optimization where a connection to the AI model is pre-established at startup so requests can be answered faster when they arrive.

This file implements the review task feature, which allows the system to analyze code or content through a specialized review agent. It launches a separate AI conversation with review-specific rules (like disabling web search and requiring structured output), processes the review results, and communicates back to the user with findings and explanations.

This file implements the undo functionality that allows users to revert their last code generation action. When triggered, it finds the most recent snapshot of code changes, restores the repository to that state, and notifies the user of the outcome through status messages.

This file handles the execution of shell commands that users run directly within the Codex environment. It takes a command string, runs it in the user's default shell (supporting features like pipes and redirects), captures the output, and reports back the results—including success/failure status, exit codes, and stdout/stderr—to the session so the user sees what happened.

This file detects what terminal application the user is running Codex in and generates a standardized identifier for it. The system checks environment variables in a prioritized order to identify the specific terminal (like iTerm, VS Code, kitty, etc.), its version, and whether a terminal multiplexer like tmux is active. This information is then packaged into a user-friendly label for logging and analytics, and used to configure the interface appropriately for that terminal's capabilities.

This file provides testing utilities that let engineers write integration tests across different parts of the codebase without needing to set up real external services or complex configurations. It exposes simplified ways to create test versions of the core components (like authentication and model management) that the product relies on.

This file handles converting text output from shell commands into readable text, even when that output uses legacy character encodings from older operating systems. When developers run commands through VS Code on Windows, they often get output encoded in formats like Windows-1251 (Cyrillic) or CP866 (Russian DOS), which can appear as garbled characters; this file automatically detects the correct encoding and converts it to standard Unicode text so users see the right characters instead of gibberish. It includes special logic to handle cases where similar byte values represent different characters in different encodings (like smart quotes in Windows-1252 versus Cyrillic letters in CP866), choosing the most likely interpretation based on context.

This file manages the lifecycle of conversation threads in Codex. It creates new threads, loads previous threads from saved history, allows users to fork threads at specific points in a conversation, and keeps track of all active threads in memory. It also handles setup tasks like monitoring for skill updates and managing authentication and AI model availability for each thread.

This file manages user authentication credentials and subscription information extracted from OpenAI login tokens. It parses user details like email address, subscription plan type (Free, Plus, Pro, Team, Business, etc.), and account identifiers from authentication tokens so the product can identify who the user is and what features they have access to.

This file manages the data structures and conversions that represent tool calls and their results as they flow through the system. It defines how different types of tools (functions, custom tools, shell commands, and external integrations) package their inputs and outputs, and ensures results are properly formatted for logging and for sending back to the user. It also handles truncating large outputs for telemetry to keep logs manageable.

This file manages event reporting for Codex tool executions, tracking and communicating what happens when the AI runs commands or applies code changes. It captures the beginning and end states of three types of operations—shell commands, patch applications, and unified execution—then sends detailed notifications about success, failure, timeouts, or user rejections back to the session for display to the user.

This file handles the "apply_patch" tool, which lets the AI modify files in a codebase. It validates patch requests from the AI, executes the file changes, tracks what was modified, and optionally requires user approval before applying patches—essentially acting as a gatekeeper to safely apply the AI's proposed edits to your code.

This file manages a set of collaboration tools that allow AI agents to spawn, communicate with, and control other agents. It handles five operations: spawning new agents with initial instructions, sending input to running agents, resuming previously closed agents, waiting for agents to reach completion, and shutting down agents. The handler also enforces safety limits like maximum nesting depth and prevents inefficient polling by enforcing minimum wait timeouts.

This file handles function calls that are defined dynamically at runtime rather than built into the system. When the AI model requests to use such a tool, this handler packages up the request details and sends them to the client application, then waits to receive the results back before returning them to the model.

This file implements a file search tool that lets Codex find files matching a text pattern within a codebase. When invoked, it searches through files using a pattern (with optional filtering by file type), returns matching file paths up to a configurable limit, and reports back whether results were found—enabling the AI to locate relevant code during its reasoning process.

This file enables the AI agent to execute JavaScript code in a sandboxed environment and report results back. It handles two operations: running arbitrary JavaScript snippets with optional timeout settings, and resetting the JavaScript runtime between executions. The code validates input to reject common mistakes (like JSON-wrapped code or markdown formatting) and tracks execution events for debugging and monitoring purposes.

This file implements a directory browser tool that allows Codex to explore a user's file system. When requested, it reads a folder, displays its contents in a hierarchical tree format with pagination controls, and returns a formatted list showing files, folders, and symlinks at varying depth levels.

This file handles the execution of Model Context Protocol (MCP) tool calls—it receives requests to run external tools through the MCP standard and returns their results back to the system. It acts as the bridge that allows Codex to invoke and manage third-party tools that follow the MCP specification.

This file handles requests to list and read resources from external services connected to Codex. It implements three operations: listing available resources, listing resource templates, and reading the contents of a specific resource—each optionally filtered by a specific service or querying across all connected services. The handler formats these results back to the AI model and emits telemetry events to track when each operation starts and completes.

This file serves as a central hub that organizes and exposes all the different tools that Codex can use to accomplish tasks—such as reading files, running shell commands, searching code, applying patches, and getting user input. It acts as a switchboard that makes these capabilities available to the rest of the system.

This file implements the "update plan" tool that allows the AI to document and communicate its work plan to users. When the AI wants to record progress on a task—such as listing steps, marking them as pending, in progress, or completed—this file handles that communication and ensures the plan information is formatted and sent to clients for display.

This file enables Codex to read and extract portions of source code files intelligently. It supports two reading modes: a simple line-range mode for fetching specific sections of a file, and a smarter indentation-aware mode that understands code structure to return complete logical blocks (like functions or conditionals) along with their parent contexts. Users can specify start line, number of lines, indentation depth limits, and whether to include sibling blocks or header comments—allowing the AI to retrieve meaningful code context rather than arbitrary line ranges.

This file implements a tool that lets the AI ask users clarifying questions and wait for their answers. It's only available in Plan mode and validates that questions include answer options before sending them to the user.

This file implements a search feature that helps users find the right tool or integration to use by matching their natural language query against available tools. When a user searches for something like "send email" or "create calendar event," this component searches through all available tools—including third-party app integrations—and returns the most relevant matches ranked by relevance score.

This file handles the execution of shell commands when Codex needs to run terminal operations. It receives command requests from the AI model, validates them for safety and permission requirements, and then executes them in a sandboxed environment while tracking the results. It also prevents certain dangerous operations from running if security policies don't permit them.

This file implements a testing tool that helps verify synchronization behavior in the system by allowing multiple parallel operations to coordinate with each other using barriers and optional delays. It enables product testing scenarios where different parts of the system need to wait for each other to reach specific points before proceeding.

This file handles the execution of terminal commands and input handling for Codex. It receives requests from the AI model to run shell commands or send keyboard input to running processes, validates security permissions, checks whether commands are safe to auto-execute, and returns the command output and execution details back to the model.

This file handles the "view image" feature, allowing Codex to load and display images from a user's computer when the AI model supports image inputs. It validates that images exist, attaches them to the conversation for the AI to see, and reports success or errors back to the user.

This file implements a JavaScript REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) execution engine that allows AI models to run JavaScript code in an isolated Node.js environment. It manages a long-running JavaScript kernel process, sends code execution requests to it, handles results, and enables the JavaScript code to call back into other tools available to the system—all while enforcing security restrictions like preventing recursive calls and blocking sensitive imports.

This file provides the formatting and organization layer for the tools system that executes code and commands. It prepares execution results (output, exit codes, timing information) into human-readable formats that can be sent back to the AI model, with built-in safeguards to truncate overly large responses and indicate when results have been cut off for brevity.

This file manages the approval and execution workflow for tool calls. It handles three key steps in sequence: asking the user for permission to run a tool, choosing an appropriate security sandbox level, and retrying with fewer restrictions if the initial attempt fails—all without asking for approval twice.

This file manages the execution of tool calls (like running code or executing commands) within a conversation session. It handles running multiple tools in parallel when allowed, serializing them when necessary, and gracefully stopping them if the user cancels the request.

This file manages how Codex discovers and executes tools (like shell commands, functions, or external integrations). It maintains a registry of available tools, routes tool requests to the appropriate handler, tracks whether tools will modify the user's system, and records what happened after each tool runs for monitoring and extensibility purposes.

This file acts as the central dispatcher that manages all tool calls in Codex—it receives requests to use a tool, validates them, and routes them to the appropriate handler. It also enforces access policies, like preventing certain tools from being called directly when the system is configured to only allow tool use through the JavaScript environment.

This file handles the execution of code patches that have already been approved by the system. It takes an approved patch request, verifies it has proper permissions, asks the user for final confirmation if needed, and then safely runs the patch application in an isolated environment while streaming output back to the user.

This file manages how different types of tools execute commands in sandboxed environments. It provides helper functions to safely prepare and execute commands—such as converting command arguments into a standardized format, and intelligently wrapping shell commands to preserve environment state (like saved shell configuration) when running them. The file serves as the foundation for multiple tool runtimes (patch application, shell commands, and unified execution) to reuse common logic for approvals, sandboxing, and retries.

This file handles the execution of shell commands within Codex. It manages the approval workflow (asking users to review potentially risky commands), applies security sandboxing rules, and then runs the command in a controlled environment with appropriate permissions and network access.

This file handles the approval and execution of unified command requests in Codex. It checks whether a command needs user approval, enforces sandboxing rules, and then delegates to the process manager to actually run the command in a terminal.

This file defines the approval and sandboxing framework that controls when and how Codex runs code. It manages user consent for tool execution—deciding whether to ask for approval, allow execution in a protected sandbox, or block it entirely—and caches approval decisions so users aren't repeatedly prompted for similar operations.

# Summary

This file defines and configures the set of tools available to AI agents in Codex. It translates feature flags and model configurations into a concrete list of capabilities—like running shell commands, reading files, searching the web, spawning sub-agents, and integrating with external services via MCP—and ensures each tool has the right parameters and descriptions for the AI to use them correctly.

This file manages text truncation for AI model outputs when they exceed size limits. It intelligently shortens long text by preserving the beginning and end while removing the middle, then inserts a marker showing what was cut. It supports truncation based on either character/byte count or token count (an approximation of how much context the AI model uses), and handles special cases like function outputs that contain mixed text and images.

This file tracks file changes during a code generation turn and produces a unified diff (like Git diffs) showing all modifications made to the codebase. It captures the initial state of files before any edits, monitors subsequent changes through file additions, updates, and deletions, and generates a consolidated diff that shows what changed from start to finish—including handling file renames, binary files, and symlinks. The diff output is formatted in a Git-compatible style so developers can easily see what the AI generated.

This file captures and packages context about the user's workspace—including the current Git repository state, commit history, and any uncommitted changes—to send along with requests to the AI. It intelligently gathers this information both immediately and in the background, so the AI model can better understand the codebase it's working with.

This file handles the real-time streaming of command output back to users. It continuously monitors a running terminal process, captures its output in small chunks, and sends those updates to the user as they happen—similar to watching a command run in a terminal window. It also waits for the process to finish and sends a final summary event with the complete output and exit status.

This file defines error messages that occur when Codex attempts to run code or commands in a sandboxed execution environment. It covers issues like failing to start a process, losing the input connection, commands being blocked by security policies, and other execution failures—helping the system communicate what went wrong when code execution requests don't succeed.

This file implements a smart output buffer that keeps the beginning and end of code execution output while automatically discarding the middle when the output gets too large. It's used to show users the start and finish of their code's results without storing everything in memory, which is especially useful for long-running processes that produce lots of output.

This file manages interactive command execution with built-in safeguards—it orchestrates running commands in isolated environments, handles user approvals, buffers output, and lets processes persist across multiple requests so users can maintain stateful shell sessions.

This file manages the lifecycle and output handling of code execution processes that run in sandboxed environments. It captures everything a running process outputs, buffers it for retrieval, detects when the process finishes, and can terminate execution or detect if the sandbox blocked the operation—helping the system understand whether code ran successfully or hit permission restrictions.

This file manages the lifecycle of running shell commands and processes in Codex. It handles starting new command sessions, collecting their output within time limits, routing input back to running processes, and cleaning up old sessions when resource limits are reached. It also enforces environment defaults (like disabling colored output) and implements a strategy to prune the oldest or already-exited processes when too many accumulate.

This file formats the results of shell commands that users execute so they can be stored and shared in conversations. It takes command execution details—like what was run, whether it succeeded, how long it took, and what output it produced—and wraps them in a structured text format that the system can recognize and pass along to AI models for context.

This utility file provides helper functions that support core product features: it enables retry logic with exponential backoff for failed requests, extracts and formats error messages from server responses for display to users, constructs resume commands so users can continue interrupted conversations by thread name or ID, and captures structured feedback metadata (like model version or cache status) for product analytics.

This file formats web search actions into human-readable descriptions that can be displayed to users—for example, converting a search operation into a summary like "query ..." or showing which URL is being opened or searched within.

This file manages Windows sandbox security settings for Codex, determining whether code execution runs in a restricted sandbox environment and at what security level. It handles reading sandbox preferences from configuration files, supports both new and legacy configuration formats, and provides functions to initialize sandbox setup on Windows machines.
This file manages read permissions for directories in Codex's sandboxed Windows environment, validating that requested folders exist and are properly formatted before granting access to them.
This file serves as the main entry point for the codebase's integration tests, organizing all test modules in one place so they can be run together as a complete test suite.

This file provides testing utilities and helpers for the Codex test suite. It sets up isolated test environments, loads test data, waits for application events during testing, and offers convenience functions for developers to write tests that work across different operating systems and network conditions—essentially a shared toolkit so tests can be written consistently and reliably.

This file provides testing utilities to monitor and manage background processes during tests—it allows tests to wait for a process to start (by reading its ID from a file), check if a process is still running, and wait for a process to shut down cleanly.

This file provides testing infrastructure for mocking and validating API responses in the Codex product. It creates fake servers and request recorders that let developers test how the code handles different response scenarios—including streaming responses, WebSocket connections, function calls, and tool outputs—without hitting real APIs. The file also validates that requests follow expected patterns, catching bugs early by ensuring things like function call outputs always match their corresponding function calls.

This file provides a test server that mimics the streaming behavior of Codex's response API. It listens for HTTP requests and serves pre-configured response streams using Server-Sent Events (SSE), with the ability to pause and resume individual chunks—useful for testing how the client handles slow or interrupted streams.

This file provides testing infrastructure for Codex, allowing engineers to create and control realistic test environments where the AI assistant can be exercised and verified. It offers builders that spin up temporary workspaces, configure mock API servers, submit prompts to the assistant, and inspect what requests were sent to those servers—enabling tests to verify that Codex behaves correctly end-to-end.

This file provides testing utilities that set up isolated, temporary environments for running tests of the Codex execution engine. It creates fake home directories and working directories, configures test credentials, and optionally connects tests to a mock server so engineers can verify the Codex executor behaves correctly without hitting real APIs.

This file tests that Codex correctly attaches the right metadata headers to outgoing requests when communicating with AI models. It verifies that features like sub-agent identification, reasoning summaries, and workspace information (Git repository details) are properly included in request headers, ensuring the backend receives complete context about where requests come from and what environment they're operating in.

This file tests the interrupt feature that allows users to stop long-running tasks in Codex. It verifies three key behaviors: that interrupting a task properly stops it and signals completion, that the system remembers what was interrupted and reports it to the AI model on the next turn, and that a marker is added to the conversation history so the model knows a turn was aborted.

This file contains automated tests that verify Codex's ability to communicate with the API over a real-time WebSocket connection. The tests simulate various scenarios—like executing shell commands, handling delays, and testing different API versions—to ensure the agent can properly send requests and receive responses through this channel.

This file is a comprehensive test suite that verifies the "apply patch" feature—a tool that lets Codex automatically modify files in a user's workspace by applying structured code changes. The tests cover success cases (creating, modifying, moving, and deleting files), error handling (invalid patches, missing files, path traversal attacks), edge cases (multiple changes in one patch, renaming without content changes), and integration scenarios (patches delivered via shell commands, multiple sequential patches, and diff reporting to the user).

This file tests how Codex handles user approval for potentially risky operations like running commands, writing files, and applying patches. It validates that the system correctly prompts for approval based on different approval policies (like "ask on request" or "ask unless trusted"), sandbox restrictions (like read-only mode or workspace-only access), and permission levels. The test suite runs dozens of scenarios to ensure that when a user approves an action once in a session, the system remembers that approval for similar future actions, and that denied actions are properly blocked.

This file tests the authentication token refresh system—verifying that the product correctly renews expired login credentials, handles refresh failures appropriately, and recovers from authentication errors. It confirms that fresh tokens are cached properly, stale tokens trigger automatic refresh, permanent failures (like expired credentials) are reported correctly, temporary server errors are retried, and the system can recover from unauthorized access by reloading saved credentials and refreshing them.

This file contains automated tests that verify the command-line interface works correctly when users run Codex commands. It tests key scenarios like streaming responses from AI models, applying custom instructions, saving conversation history to files, tracking git repository information, and resuming previous conversations—ensuring the CLI behaves as expected from a user's perspective.

This file is a comprehensive test suite that verifies how the Codex client sends requests to AI model APIs and processes responses. It tests critical product features like authentication, conversation history management, user instructions, model configuration options (reasoning effort, verbosity), rate limiting, error handling, and resuming saved conversations—essentially ensuring that user inputs are properly formatted and transmitted to the backend with all necessary context and settings.

This file contains integration tests for Codex's WebSocket-based communication with AI models. It verifies that the product correctly establishes WebSocket connections, sends requests, receives streaming responses, handles errors, manages rate limits, tracks performance metrics, and intelligently reuses connections across multiple conversation turns. The tests ensure features like connection pre-warming, timing instrumentation, and continuation of previous responses all work as expected.

This file tests how Codex handles approval workflows when delegating tasks to sub-agents. It verifies that when a sub-agent requests permission to execute commands or apply code changes, the parent system properly surfaces those approval requests to the user, collects their decision, and communicates the result back so the sub-agent can proceed or stop accordingly.

This file tests how Codex handles collaboration instructions—custom guidelines that can be set to control how the AI assistant behaves during interactions. It verifies that instructions are correctly included in requests sent to the AI model, properly updated when changed, and persist across session resumption, while avoiding duplication when instructions remain unchanged.

# Summary

This file contains integration tests for Codex's conversation context compaction feature—the ability to summarize long conversation histories to stay within token limits. It tests both manual compaction (user-triggered) and automatic compaction (triggered when token usage exceeds a threshold), ensuring summaries are generated correctly, included in subsequent requests, and persisted in the session log. The tests also cover edge cases like model switching, retry logic on errors, and remote compaction via API endpoints.

# compact_remote.rs

This file contains integration tests that verify the remote conversation history compression feature works correctly. When conversations get too long, the system can send the full history to a remote service to compress it into a summary, replacing the original messages to save space and stay within token limits. The tests check that this compression happens automatically when needed, that it correctly replaces old messages with summaries in follow-up requests, that it smartly trims older interactions while keeping recent ones, and that it properly recovers fresh system instructions after compression.

This file tests that conversation history is correctly maintained when users compact, resume, and fork AI conversations. It simulates real scenarios—like condensing a long chat, picking up where you left off, or branching a conversation at an earlier point—and verifies that what the AI model sees matches what's expected at each step.

This file tests that the product correctly alerts users when they're using outdated configuration settings or feature flags. It verifies that when users enable deprecated options (like old feature names or removed config fields), the system sends them helpful messages explaining what they should use instead and how to update their setup.

This file tests the code execution engine on macOS, verifying that commands run safely within a sandboxed environment with proper access controls. It checks that basic commands work, output is captured correctly, file writes are blocked as expected, and other sandbox restrictions are enforced.

This file tests the execution policy system that controls what commands Codex is allowed to run. It verifies that policies work correctly—blocking forbidden commands, handling edge cases like empty or whitespace-only scripts, and ensuring the system behaves properly when collaboration mode is enabled.

This test verifies that users can branch off from a conversation at an earlier point by "forking" the thread, and that forking multiple times correctly rewinds the conversation history to the desired message. Specifically, it checks that when you fork a conversation that has three messages, you can create a branch with only the first message, and then fork that branch again to confirm the history is correctly truncated each time.

This file tests whether Codex can successfully use a file search tool to find matching patterns in files. It verifies that the AI correctly filters results by file type, handles search parameters, and reports when no matches are found—ensuring the feature works as intended before deployment.

This file tests a feature that allows Codex to read special AGENTS.md files from a project and automatically include their instructions when communicating with the AI model, helping organize multi-agent behavior within a codebase. The tests verify that these agent instructions are properly appended to the system prompts sent to the AI, both when a custom AGENTS.md file exists and when it doesn't.

This file tests that when users share images with Codex—either by pasting local image files or dragging and dropping images—the system correctly records and formats those interactions in its internal transaction log. It verifies that images are properly attached to user messages and preserved in the expected structure for future reference or debugging.

This file contains automated tests that verify how Codex handles different types of conversation items—such as user messages, AI responses, reasoning traces, web searches, and plans—during interactions. The tests ensure that these items are properly created, streamed, and reported to the user interface with correct metadata and content formatting.

This file tests that Codex can request and return structured data in a specific format (JSON) when a user provides a template for the expected output shape. It verifies the feature works across different AI models by simulating a user asking a question and confirming the response matches the requested format.

This file contains automated tests for a "list directory" tool that Codex will use to explore file system folders. The tests verify that when the AI agent requests to see what files and folders exist in a directory, it receives the correct list—with support for features like pagination (showing only a slice of results) and depth control (limiting how many folder levels deep to show). All tests are currently disabled pending the feature's full enablement.

This file contains optional tests that verify the Codex command-line tool works correctly when connected to the real OpenAI API. These tests are skipped by default in automated builds (to keep things fast and free) but developers can run them locally with a valid API key to confirm the tool can successfully execute user requests like creating files or running shell commands.

This file tests whether Codex properly detects and reloads custom skills (user-defined code snippets) when they change on disk. It creates a skill, submits a request that uses it, then modifies the skill file and verifies that the updated version is used in the next request instead of the cached original.

This file serves as a central hub that organizes and runs all of Codex's integration tests—which verify that different features work correctly end-to-end. It also sets up a temporary test environment so that tests can safely simulate how Codex behaves without affecting the developer's actual system files.

This file tests that the system correctly applies configuration overrides to AI model settings, particularly verifying that custom limits for tool output can override default model behavior when querying model information.

This file contains automated tests that verify how the Codex system handles temporary model overrides. Specifically, it ensures that when users temporarily switch to a different AI model (like changing from GPT-4o to o3) for a single conversation, those changes don't accidentally get saved to the user's configuration file or create new configuration files.

This file tests how Codex handles switching between different AI models during a conversation. It verifies that when users change models, the system properly updates instructions, strips unsupported content (like images when switching to text-only models), adjusts context window sizes, and maintains conversation continuity.

This file tests that different AI models have access to the correct set of capabilities (tools) they're supposed to use. It verifies that when you interact with each model version—like GPT-5, GPT-5.1, or GPT-5.1-Codex—the system correctly enables the right features, such as file editing, web search, resource management, and shell command execution, while keeping these capabilities consistent as the product evolves.

This file tests how the system manages caching of AI models to improve performance and reduce unnecessary network requests. It verifies that the product correctly refreshes its local cache of available models based on version changes and server-provided signals, while also validating that stale cached models can still be used when the server confirms nothing has changed.

This test file verifies that Codex correctly manages cached lists of available AI models by checking for updates when the server signals a change. Specifically, it ensures that when the server's model catalog changes (detected via an ETag header), Codex refreshes its local copy exactly once and doesn't unnecessarily fetch it again on subsequent requests with the same update signal.

This file contains automated tests that verify Codex properly records telemetry and diagnostic information about how the system processes AI responses. The tests check that key events—like API calls, tool executions, user approvals, and errors—are accurately logged with the correct metadata so engineers can monitor and debug the system's behavior in production.

This file tests that Codex correctly ignores certain configuration changes (like approval permissions, working directory, and collaboration instructions) when they're submitted outside of an active conversation turn—ensuring these overrides only take effect when there's an actual user interaction to apply them to.

This file tests whether the system correctly handles user input that arrives while a previous response is still being generated. Specifically, it verifies that when a user submits a follow-up message during an ongoing conversation turn, the next request to the AI model includes both the original message and the new follow-up message in the correct order.

This file tests that the system correctly communicates permissions and security policies to the AI model in the right situations. It verifies that permission instructions are sent when a session starts, added when security settings change, omitted when settings stay the same, and properly replayed or accumulated when sessions are resumed or branched—ensuring the AI always has current information about what it's allowed to do.

This file tests the "personality" feature of Codex, which allows users to configure how the AI assistant communicates with them (e.g., "friendly" vs. "pragmatic" tone). The tests verify that personality settings are correctly applied to instructions sent to the AI model, that personality changes during a conversation trigger appropriate notification messages, that the feature can be toggled on and off, and that personality templates work correctly whether they come from local configuration or remote model definitions.

This file tests the personality migration feature, which automatically assigns a default conversational personality to users' settings when they have existing conversation history but haven't explicitly chosen a personality style. It verifies that the migration runs only once, respects user-defined personality choices, and correctly handles various edge cases like archived sessions and incomplete data.

This is an automated test that verifies Codex properly handles a specific error scenario: when a user exceeds their API usage quota, the system should emit exactly one error message to the user rather than multiple redundant ones. It simulates this quota-exceeded situation and confirms the expected behavior.

This is a test file that verifies the "read file" feature works correctly—specifically, that when a user asks Codex to inspect a file, it can successfully retrieve and return specific lines from that file. The test is currently disabled pending full implementation of this file-reading capability.

This file contains automated tests that verify Codex's ability to fetch and use AI models from remote servers. This file contains automated tests that verify Codex properly compresses requests to the API backend when a compression feature is enabled, and that it correctly skips compression for certain authentication types. This file tests whether Codex properly handles user approval requests during code generation. It verifies that when the AI needs confirmation from the user (like "Should I proceed with this plan?"), the system captures the question, sends it to the user, receives their answer, and passes it back to the AI—and that this feature only works in specific collaboration modes where it's appropriate.

This file tests the session resume feature—verifying that when users close and reopen a Codex conversation, the system correctly restores the conversation history, preserves instructions and settings, and handles model switching without duplicating information. This test file verifies that Codex alerts users when they resume a conversation that was previously run with a different AI model than the one currently configured. It simulates resuming an old conversation with a model mismatch and confirms that an appropriate warning message appears to the user.

This file contains automated tests for the code review feature in Codex. It verifies that when a user requests a code review, the system correctly spawns a separate review session, processes the AI model's feedback (whether structured findings or plain text), prevents internal streaming details from leaking to the UI, and properly integrates the review results back into the main conversation thread. The tests also confirm that review requests respect configuration settings like custom review models and that they work correctly with overridden file paths and git-based review targets.

This file tests macOS security sandboxing features to ensure that file access restrictions are properly enforced when running code. This file tests the shell command execution feature, verifying that Codex can successfully run terminal commands and capture their output correctly. This file tests how Codex formats the output from shell commands and file-patching operations under different scenarios. This file tests a feature called "Shell Snapshot" that captures the shell environment (like environment variables, aliases, and settings) before running commands, allowing those commands to execute with a consistent, reproducible shell state. This file tests how the product handles "skills"—reusable code templates or instructions that users can reference and the AI can use when responding. This file contains automated tests that verify the system correctly saves and retrieves conversation data in a local database. This test verifies that Codex can recover and continue working after encountering an error during a response stream. This file tests that Codex properly handles interrupted data streams from the AI model provider. When the server stops sending data mid-response (before completing), the system should automatically retry the request and eventually succeed—verifying that incomplete responses don't cause the product to fail or get stuck.

This file tests Codex's ability to correctly interpret text output from shell commands on Windows, which often arrives in legacy character encodings (like Cyrillic or Latin-1) rather than modern UTF-8. It verifies that the system can automatically detect and convert these older encodings into readable text, so users see properly formatted output instead of garbled characters or question marks.

This file contains integration tests that verify Codex's ability to execute tools—like running shell commands, updating project plans, and applying code patches—and confirm that results are properly communicated back to users. It simulates real-world scenarios where the AI assistant performs actions and validates that the system handles both successful operations and error cases correctly.

This file tests whether Codex can run multiple tools in parallel to speed up execution. It verifies that when the AI asks to perform several tasks at once (like reading files or running shell commands), the system actually executes them simultaneously rather than one after another, and that results are properly grouped together before sending back to the AI.

This file contains automated tests that verify how Codex handles various tool execution scenarios—specifically shell commands, custom tools, and permission controls. It ensures that features like sandbox restrictions, timeout handling, permission escalation policies, and error reporting work correctly end-to-end by simulating conversations with the AI model and validating its responses.

This file tests how Codex handles oversized tool outputs to prevent overwhelming the AI model. This file tests that Codex properly maintains conversation state within a single turn and clears it when starting a new turn. This file tests the **undo feature** for Codex — the ability to revert the last AI-generated code changes back to their previous state. The tests also confirm that undo properly consumes saved snapshots and fails gracefully when no previous state is available to restore.

This file manages the terminal output for a debug client tool, handling how messages and prompts are displayed to users.
