This file handles incoming messages from the Codex server during a debugging session. It reads server responses and requests, automatically approves or declines certain actions (like command execution or file changes) based on settings, tracks the status of active threads, and formats server activity into human-readable output for display to the user.

This file maintains the debug client's in-memory state, tracking which requests are currently waiting for responses, what thread is being debugged, and what threads are available—essentially keeping a snapshot of the debugging session's current status.

This file is the entry point for a wrapper tool that executes code on Unix-based systems—it either runs the actual execution wrapper on Unix machines or shows an error message if someone tries to run it on a non-Unix system like Windows.

This file is the entry point for a code execution server that only runs on Unix-based systems (Linux, macOS, etc.); it exits with an error message if someone tries to run it on Windows or other non-Unix platforms.

This file serves as the entry point that exposes the core execution capabilities of the server on Unix-based systems, making functions for running code and managing communication available to other parts of the product.

This file implements a security system that safely runs shell commands with fine-grained permission control. It intercepts every command a user tries to run, checks it against a policy ruleset to decide whether to allow it, block it, or ask the user for permission, and can escalate privileges when needed—all while keeping potentially dangerous commands sandboxed and monitored.

This file handles the client-side communication for privilege escalation requests on Unix-like systems. When code needs to run a command with elevated permissions, this file communicates with a privileged server to either execute the command directly with those permissions, execute it locally, or deny the request—then returns the appropriate result back to the caller.

This file defines the communication protocol between Codex's execution server and the shells running user commands. It specifies the messages exchanged when a shell needs approval to run a command—the shell asks "can I execute this?", the server responds with "run it", "escalate it to me", or "block it", and then they exchange file descriptors and exit status information to complete the execution.

This file implements a security gatekeeper for running system commands within Codex. It receives execution requests from sandboxed processes, checks them against a security policy to determine if they should be allowed, blocked, or escalated with elevated privileges, and then executes or denies the command accordingly.
This file defines a decision-making component that determines whether to allow or deny code execution requests from users, based on the file being run, its arguments, and the working directory.

This file implements the shell command execution service that Codex exposes to AI models. It accepts requests to run bash commands with specified working directories and timeouts, executes them safely according to sandbox policies, and returns the command output, exit code, and execution time. The file also handles sandbox configuration updates from clients and advertises the execution capabilities available to connected AI assistants.

This file enforces security rules for when the AI agent tries to run system commands. It checks a policy to decide whether each command should be allowed, blocked, or prompted to the user—and if user approval is needed, it sends a question through the messaging system asking for permission to execute the command.

This file handles inter-process communication by sending and receiving messages between different parts of the system over Unix sockets, including the ability to pass file descriptors (like open files or pipes) along with those messages. It provides two main communication patterns: stream-based messaging for structured data and datagram-based messaging for raw bytes, both supporting optional file descriptor attachment.

This file implements a timer that enforces execution time limits on code operations while allowing those operations to pause the timer when they're waiting for something (like a network request). The stopwatch supports multiple overlapping pauses and automatically resumes counting only when all pauses are lifted, ensuring that the time limit reflects actual work time rather than idle waiting time.
This file serves as the entry point for running all integration tests—it loads and aggregates test cases from separate modules so they can be executed together as a single test suite.

This file provides test utilities for the execution server, enabling test code to simulate a complete Codex environment with sandboxing rules and user interactions. It sets up a mock server connection, configures sandbox policies (read-only or writable), and simulates how a user would approve or decline system requests during code execution.

This file tests whether the system properly handles user approval flows when a security policy requires prompting before running certain commands. Specifically, it verifies that when a user approves a prompt to run a restricted command (like `git init`), the command executes successfully outside the sandbox with elevated privileges.

This file tests that the execution server correctly exposes a "shell command" tool with the right description, parameters, and requirements when a product asks for its list of available tools—verifying that users will see accurate information about what the tool can do and what inputs it needs.
This file organizes automated tests for the execution server, enabling the team to verify that core features like tool discovery and user interaction work correctly on macOS and Linux systems.

This file defines the command-line interface for Codex, specifying all the options and arguments users can pass when running the tool from the terminal. It handles everything from basic settings like which AI model to use, to safety controls like sandboxing modes, to advanced features like resuming past sessions and running code reviews. It also parses and validates user input to ensure flags are used correctly together.

This file defines the interface for processing events and status updates as Codex runs, and handles saving the agent's final output message to a file. It allows different parts of the system to respond to what the agent does during execution—like displaying configuration details, reacting to each step of work, and capturing the final result.

This file formats and displays the output of Codex operations to users in the terminal. It processes events from the AI agent's execution—like commands being run, files being updated, tools being called, and reasoning steps—and prints them in a human-readable format with colors, timestamps, and appropriate emphasis to help users follow what's happening.

This file converts internal execution events into a human-readable output stream that tracks what the AI agent is doing in real time. It takes low-level system events (like commands being executed, tools being called, files being modified) and transforms them into a structured event feed that shows the user the agent's progress—such as when tasks start and finish, what commands ran, whether they succeeded, and what changes were made to files.

This file defines the event types that Codex emits to report what the agent is doing in real time. When a user interacts with Codex, the system sends back a stream of events describing each step—like when the agent starts thinking, runs a command, edits files, searches the web, or completes a task—so the UI and other consumers can track progress and display updates to the user.

This file is the entry point for Codex's command-line execution engine. It initializes the application, loads configuration and authentication settings, handles user input (prompts, images, or code review requests), and orchestrates communication between the user and the AI system—managing approvals, sandbox policies, and displaying results either as human-readable text or machine-readable JSON.

This file serves as the starting point for the `codex-exec` command-line tool, handling how users invoke it and routing their request to the appropriate functionality—either running Codex normally or running a sandboxed command execution environment depending on how the program was invoked.

This file serves as the entry point for running all of the product's integration tests—it collects test modules from across the codebase so they can be executed together to verify that the system works end-to-end.

This is a test file that validates how the system converts low-level execution events into user-facing thread events for display. It ensures that when Codex performs actions—like running commands, searching the web, calling tools, or spawning collaborating agents—those raw events are properly transformed and tracked with consistent IDs, statuses, and sequencing so users see a coherent narrative of what happened in their session.

This file contains automated tests that verify the `--add-dir` command-line flag works correctly, ensuring users can specify additional directories to include when running Codex commands, both as a single flag and repeated multiple times.

This file contains automated tests that verify the patch-application feature works correctly. Specifically, it tests whether Codex can successfully receive patch instructions (like "add this file" or "update this code") and apply those changes to a user's codebase—including handling both structured and freeform patch formats.

This file tests that the Codex executor correctly reads and uses an API authentication key from an environment variable when making requests to the Codex service. It verifies the product can properly authenticate itself with the backend without requiring manual key configuration.

This file tests whether Codex properly handles user session data storage—specifically, it verifies that sessions are saved to disk by default but can be discarded immediately when users run the tool in "ephemeral" mode, leaving no trace of their work.

This test file verifies that Codex properly exits with an error when a required plugin server (MCP server) fails to start up. It simulates a broken plugin configuration and confirms the application catches the failure and reports it clearly to the user instead of silently continuing.
This file serves as an index that organizes and groups together the product's integration tests, making it easier to run and manage all the end-to-end testing scenarios in one place.

This file tests that the Codex command-line tool correctly identifies itself when communicating with the server, and that users can override this identification if needed for testing or debugging purposes. It verifies both the default behavior and the ability to customize how the tool reports its origin.

This test verifies that when a user provides an output schema file, the system correctly includes that schema in requests sent to the AI model, ensuring the model knows what format to return results in.

This file tests the "resume session" feature of Codex, which allows users to continue previous conversations. The tests verify that users can pick up where they left off by resuming their most recent session, resuming a specific session by ID, and that resumed sessions properly preserve configuration settings, accept new prompts and images, and correctly filter sessions by working directory.

This file tests that Codex's sandboxing features work correctly on Unix systems (macOS and Linux). It verifies that code can be safely executed in isolated environments with restricted file system and network access, ensuring that processes stay within their intended boundaries—for example, that Python multiprocessing locks work properly in a sandbox, that certain system calls are allowed, and that write permissions are enforced correctly based on sandbox policies.

This test file verifies that when the Codex server reports an error, the command-line tool exits with an error status code so that automated scripts and systems can reliably detect when something went wrong.
This file tells the build system to regenerate the code whenever the default policy configuration file changes, ensuring the product always uses the latest execution policy settings.

This file defines a catalog of patterns that the system uses to validate command-line arguments before execution. It specifies what types of inputs are acceptable (like file paths, integers, or literal text) and how many arguments each pattern should match, helping the execution policy enforce security and correctness rules for commands.

This file validates and maps command-line arguments against expected patterns, ensuring the right arguments are provided in the right positions. It's used by the execution policy system to verify that user-supplied commands match what the product expects before allowing them to run.

This file defines the categories that Codex uses to understand what type of information each command-line argument represents—whether it's a literal value, a file to read, a file to write, a number, or something else. It validates that arguments match their expected type (for example, checking that a positive integer argument is actually a number) and helps the system understand which arguments might modify files, which is important for security and understanding command behavior.

This file defines the error messages that the system reports when users provide invalid command-line arguments or when the system detects unsafe operations. It serves as a central catalog of all the ways things can go wrong—such as missing required options, unexpected arguments, or attempts to access files outside allowed directories—so that meaningful feedback can be displayed to users.

This file defines the structure for representing a single program execution request—storing which program to run and what arguments to pass to it—so that execution calls can be created, displayed to users, and transmitted between system components.

This file enforces security rules for command execution by validating that file paths referenced in commands are within approved folders. It checks that files marked as readable are in permitted read locations and files marked as writable are in permitted write locations, then returns the path to the actual executable program to run.

This file acts as the main entry point for a policy enforcement system that validates whether programs and their command-line arguments are allowed to run. It bundles together various internal components (like argument parsing, policy checking, and execution validation) and exposes them as a public API, while also providing a built-in default security policy that other parts of the product can load.

This file is a command-line tool that checks whether a program command is safe to run based on a security policy. It accepts a command (either as raw arguments or JSON), evaluates it against a policy file, and outputs a result indicating whether the command is safe, forbidden, or couldn't be verified—each with a specific exit code that the rest of the system can use to make decisions.

This file defines the data structures that represent command-line options (like `-h` or `--help`) that the Codex system can accept and parse. It specifies whether an option is a simple flag, requires a value, and whether it's mandatory—allowing the rest of the codebase to understand what arguments users can provide when running the tool.

This file enforces execution policies by checking whether code generation requests should be allowed or blocked. It validates incoming execution calls against a set of rules—including forbidden programs, forbidden text patterns, and program-specific policies—and returns either approval or a rejection reason. It also provides methods to verify that the policy rules themselves are correctly configured.

This file reads and interprets policy configuration files that define which programs are allowed to run and what arguments they can accept. It parses human-readable policy rules (like "allow program X with these flags and arguments") into an internal format the system can enforce, while also tracking forbidden programs and dangerous text patterns that should be blocked.

This file defines the core policy engine that decides whether a command is allowed to run. It parses command-line arguments against a set of predefined rules (like which options are required, which arguments are valid) and either approves the command or blocks it with a reason. It also validates the policy rules themselves by testing them against known good and bad command examples.

This file validates whether a `sed` command (a text-processing instruction) is safe to execute by checking if it matches a known safe pattern—specifically, commands that print lines between two line numbers, like "122,202p". If the command doesn't match this safe pattern, it rejects it to prevent potentially unsafe operations.

This file defines the data structure that represents a command that has been approved to run by the system's security policy. It captures what program will execute, what flags and options were provided, what arguments were passed, and optionally suggests safer system paths to use instead of searching the user's environment. The file also includes a method to check whether the approved command might write files to disk, which helps the system understand the potential side effects of allowing the command to run.

This file sets up the test framework for the execution policy module by collecting all test cases from a separate test suite folder, allowing the team to run comprehensive validation checks on the codebase in one go.

This file contains automated tests that verify the system correctly rejects code examples that should be flagged as problematic or unsafe. It ensures that Codex's security policy catches bad code patterns as intended.

This test file verifies that the system correctly validates and approves `cp` (copy) commands based on predefined security rules, ensuring the command has the right number of arguments and proper file access permissions before allowing execution.
This file verifies that the system's default security policy correctly allows all code examples it's supposed to approve—it's a quality check ensuring the allowlist actually works as intended.

This file tests the safety validation of the `head` command—a standard Unix utility that displays the first lines of files. It verifies that the policy system correctly accepts valid `head` commands (like reading a single file or using the `-n` flag with a valid line count) and rejects invalid ones (like passing zero, floats, or negative numbers as the line count argument).

This file tests whether the policy system correctly validates command-line arguments when they must match exact literal values. Specifically, it verifies that commands are allowed when their arguments match a policy's requirements exactly, and rejected when arguments don't match what was defined.

This file tests Codex's ability to validate and safely execute the `ls` command with various combinations of flags and file arguments. It ensures that the system correctly allows safe command variations (like `ls -l -a file.txt`), rejects invalid options (like `-z`), and properly identifies which files are being accessed—a core security feature for preventing unauthorized file operations.

This file organizes a collection of integration tests that verify different aspects of the code execution system, covering scenarios like command parsing, file operations, and text processing utilities.

This file tests whether the system can correctly validate sed commands (a text-processing tool syntax) to determine if they're safe to execute, accepting properly formatted commands and rejecting ones that don't meet safety requirements.

This file tests whether the system correctly validates attempts to run the `pwd` (print working directory) command with various arguments. It verifies that the command is allowed when run with no arguments or with specific flags like `-L` or `-P`, and that it's properly rejected when given unexpected extra arguments.

This file tests whether the product safely allows or blocks execution of the `sed` command (a text editing tool) based on a security policy. It verifies that legitimate sed operations like printing specific lines are permitted, while dangerous commands that could execute arbitrary code are rejected.

This file manages the ability to add new execution rules to a policy file. It takes a command prefix (like "echo Hello, world!") and appends a corresponding allow rule to the system's policy configuration, creating necessary directories and handling file locking to prevent conflicts when multiple processes try to update the policy simultaneously.

This file defines the three possible approval states that the system can assign to a command: Allow (run freely), Prompt (ask the user), or Forbidden (block it). It handles converting text-based approval decisions into the system's internal format and ensures they're stored and transmitted correctly.

This file defines the error types and messages that the execution policy system can produce, allowing it to report problems clearly to users when policies, rules, or examples are invalid. It also captures the exact location in a policy file where errors occur so they can be highlighted to the user.

This file implements a command-line tool that checks whether a given command is allowed according to one or more security policy rule files. It loads the policy rules, evaluates the command against them, and reports back which rules matched and what decision (allow/deny) was reached—all in a readable output format.

This file serves as the main entry point for the execution policy system, organizing and exposing the core components that determine what code or commands are allowed to run. It bundles together the policy parser, rule matching, decision-making logic, and error handling so other parts of the product can easily check whether a given operation should be permitted.

This file provides a command-line tool that lets users evaluate whether a command is allowed under a specific execution policy. It serves as the entry point for the policy checking feature, accepting user input and routing it to the appropriate verification logic.

This file implements the policy parser that reads and interprets policy configuration files written in a simple scripting language. When users write policies to control what commands or programs are allowed to run, this parser processes those policy definitions, validates them (checking that examples match the intended behavior), and converts them into executable rules that the system can apply.

This file manages the rules that determine whether Codex should allow or block execution of code commands. It stores a set of rules organized by program name, checks incoming commands against those rules, and returns a decision (allow/block) along with which rules matched—falling back to a heuristic-based decision if no explicit rules apply.

This file defines the rules system that controls which commands are allowed or blocked in the execution policy. It provides a pattern-matching framework that checks incoming commands against predefined rules—each rule can match a command prefix (like "git push") and either approve or deny it, optionally with a reason explaining why the rule exists.

This file tests the policy engine that controls which commands are allowed, forbidden, or require user approval. It validates that rules are parsed correctly from policy files, matched against commands accurately, and that the system properly handles edge cases like duplicate rules, empty justifications, and conflicting decisions across multiple rules.

This file implements a feedback collection and reporting system that captures detailed diagnostic information (logs and metadata) from the Codex application and uploads it to Sentry for debugging and issue tracking. It maintains a rolling buffer of recent logs so that when users report problems, the system can attach comprehensive context about what happened, then sends that data along with user-provided classification (like "bug" or "bad result") to a centralized error monitoring service.

This file defines the command-line interface for a file search tool that lets users fuzzy-match filenames in a directory, with options to control output format, result limits, number of search threads, and exclusion patterns.

This file implements a fast, fuzzy file search engine that scans directories for files matching a search query. It powers quick-open and file-finding features by running queries in the background while continuously scanning the file system, returning ranked results that update in real-time as the user types. The search respects `.gitignore` rules by default, supports excluding specific patterns, and can optionally highlight which characters in filenames matched the query.

This file is the entry point for a command-line tool that searches for files and displays matching results. It handles how results are presented to the user—either as plain text with highlighted match positions, formatted as machine-readable data, or as a simple file list—and shows warnings when the search returns too many results to display.

This file serves as the public interface for the hooks system, which allows the product to trigger custom notifications and actions at specific points in the code execution (like after the AI uses a tool or completes an agent task). It exposes the core components that other parts of the application need to register hooks, define what events trigger them, and send notifications to users.

This file manages **hooks**—custom programs that users can configure to run automatically at specific points in Codex's workflow, such as after the AI agent responds or after a tool is used. It handles setting up these hooks from configuration, executing them in the right order, and allows them to stop further processing if needed.

This file defines the data structures and event system for **hooks**—notifications that fire when specific things happen in Codex (like when an agent completes a turn or a tool finishes executing). It standardizes what information gets captured and sent out when these events occur, so external systems can react to them (for logging, monitoring, or triggering workflows).

This file handles notifications that alert external systems when the AI agent completes a task. It packages information about what the user asked for, what the agent did, and where it happened into a message format that can be sent to user-defined notification scripts or webhooks.

This file manages secure storage and retrieval of user credentials (like API keys or passwords) by interfacing with the system's secure credential storage. It provides both a real implementation that stores credentials securely and a mock version for testing purposes.

This file sets up the build process for a sandboxing component on Linux. It compiles bubblewrap (a tool that isolates code execution in secure containers) from source code during the product build, ensuring the sandbox feature is available when Codex runs on Linux systems.

This file implements sandboxing for code execution on Linux by setting up restricted filesystem and network environments. It creates a secure sandbox where the entire filesystem is read-only by default, with only explicitly approved directories allowed to be written to, and protects sensitive folders like `.git` and `.codex` from being modified even within writable areas—similar to how the macOS sandbox works.

This file enforces security sandboxing rules on code execution in Linux environments by blocking dangerous operations like network access and restricting file system writes. It applies these restrictions to child processes so they run in a locked-down mode while the main application remains unrestricted.

This file is the entry point for Codex's Linux security sandbox, which isolates code execution by restricting what system permissions the code can access and limiting what files it can see. When Codex runs untrusted code on Linux, this component enforces those safety boundaries.

This file is the main entry point for Codex's Linux sandboxing system on Linux machines. It takes code execution requests with security policies and sets up an isolated environment where that code runs safely by controlling its file system access, network access, and system permissions. The file decides whether to use the newer bubblewrap-based isolation method or fall back to an older approach, and handles edge cases like environments that don't support certain isolation features.

This file is the entry point for a sandboxed execution environment on Linux that allows code to run in an isolated container while preserving the current working directory, environment variables, and command arguments passed to it.

This file enables Codex to run user code in an isolated sandbox environment on Linux by invoking bubblewrap (a sandboxing tool) that's compiled as part of the build process. It acts as a bridge between Codex and bubblewrap, handling the technical details of launching the sandboxed execution while providing clear error messages if sandboxing isn't properly set up.
This file serves as the entry point for the product's integration tests, organizing and running all test scenarios that verify the sandbox functionality works correctly end-to-end.

This file contains automated security tests that verify the Linux sandbox properly restricts what code can do—such as blocking network access, preventing writes to system directories, and protecting sensitive configuration files from being overwritten. It ensures that when Codex executes user code in a sandboxed environment, malicious or accidental operations are safely blocked.

This file serves as a central collection point for integration tests, organizing different test categories (like sandboxing security tests) into one place so they can all be run together as a test suite.

This file enables Codex to connect to and manage models through LM Studio, a local AI model server. It handles discovering available models, downloading new models, loading them into memory, and verifying the server is running—essentially acting as a bridge between Codex and the user's local LM Studio installation.

This file sets up local AI model support by connecting to a local LM Studio server, ensuring the required model is downloaded and ready to use when a user selects the open-source option. It handles the background setup work—checking what models are available locally, downloading missing ones, and preloading the model—so the system is prepared when the user wants to run code completion locally.

This file implements a device-code login flow that allows users to authenticate with their ChatGPT account through a browser-based code exchange. When a user runs the login command, the system generates a unique code and verification URL, displays them in the terminal with clear instructions, and then polls the server in the background until the user completes authentication in their browser—at which point login credentials are securely saved locally.

This file serves as the main entry point for user login and authentication in Codex, exposing the key features needed to log in users (like device code authentication and a local login server) along with core authentication utilities for managing API keys, tokens, and user sessions.

This file generates security codes used during the login process to protect against certain types of attacks. It creates a pair of linked codes—one that the app keeps secret and sends later, and another that's sent upfront—which work together to verify that login requests are legitimate.

# LoginServer

This file runs a lightweight web server that handles the login flow for Codex. When a user initiates login, it starts a local server, directs them to OpenAI's authentication page, waits for them to complete the login, captures the returned credentials, and saves them locally for future use. It also handles graceful shutdown and retry logic if the server port is already in use from a previous login attempt.
This file organizes and runs all the login feature tests by collecting them into a single test suite, making it easy to execute all login-related checks at once.

This file tests the device code login flow—a method that lets users authenticate by entering a code on a website rather than typing credentials directly into the app. It simulates the entire login process by mocking server responses and verifies that credentials are properly saved, workspace restrictions are enforced, and errors are handled gracefully.

This file contains automated tests that verify the login system works correctly end-to-end. It simulates a complete login flow—including a mock authentication provider, browser callback, and credential storage—to ensure users can successfully authenticate and their login credentials are properly saved and managed.
This file organizes and bundles together the integration tests that verify the login system works correctly end-to-end, including device code authentication and the login server behavior.

This file defines the configuration and interface for how external systems can interact with Codex. It specifies what parameters users can pass when starting a new Codex session (like the initial prompt, which AI model to use, approval settings for running commands, and sandbox restrictions) and how they can continue an existing conversation. It also generates the formal specifications that external tools need to understand in order to call Codex correctly.

This file manages the execution of Codex tool sessions when called through the MCP protocol. It starts a Codex session, submits user prompts to it, streams back events (like approvals for code execution or file changes) as they happen, and sends the final result back to the client when the session completes or encounters an error.

This file handles asking for user approval before Codex executes system commands. When Codex wants to run a command, this code sends a request to the user asking "Allow Codex to run `[command]` in `[directory]`?", waits for their approval or denial decision, and then reports that decision back to the Codex engine so it can proceed or stop accordingly.

This file is the entry point for an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that acts as a bridge between a user's command line and the Codex system. It sets up three coordinated tasks—reading requests from the user's input, processing those requests through the Codex engine, and sending responses back to the user—while managing configuration settings and logging along the way.

This file is the entry point that starts the MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, which enables Codex to communicate with external tools and integrations. It initializes the server with default configuration settings and prepares the system to handle requests from connected clients.

This file is the central message router for the Codex MCP server—it receives requests and notifications from external clients, decides what to do with them, and orchestrates the appropriate responses. It handles initialization, processes tool calls that start Codex sessions or continue existing conversations, manages cancellations, and routes unsupported requests back with error messages.

This file handles sending messages from the Codex server back to clients. It provides a message sender that queues outgoing requests, responses, notifications, and errors, while also managing callbacks so the server can receive replies to requests it sends to clients.

This file handles the approval workflow when Codex wants to apply code changes to your files. It sends a request to the user asking "Allow Codex to apply proposed code changes?", waits for their approval or denial decision, and then reports that decision back to the system so Codex can proceed or stop accordingly.
This file organizes the code that handles tool operations—specifically creating conversations and sending messages—by grouping these related functions into separate, manageable modules.
This file serves as the entry point for running all integration tests in the project, organizing and executing test suites that verify the server works correctly end-to-end.

This file is a testing utility library that provides helper tools and mock servers for testing the Codex system. It exposes reusable test components like a mock AI model server, a process manager for testing the Codex service, and helper functions to simulate and validate the responses the system sends back to users.

This file provides a test helper that launches and communicates with the Codex MCP server during testing. It manages the server process lifecycle, handles the initialization handshake with the server, and provides utilities for tests to send requests and read responses back from the server in a controlled way.

This file sets up a fake server for testing purposes that simulates how the real API responds to requests. When the code being tested makes requests to the `/v1/responses` endpoint, this fake server returns pre-recorded responses in the order they were provided, allowing tests to verify that the system handles API responses correctly without needing an actual running service.

This file creates test responses that simulate how the Codex server communicates results back to clients — it generates mock messages for shell commands, assistant replies, and code patches in the streaming format used by the API.

This file contains automated tests for Codex's approval and execution system. It verifies that when AI-suggested actions (like running shell commands or applying code changes) are flagged as untrusted, the system properly asks a user for permission before executing them, and that approvals work end-to-end. The tests simulate the entire flow: a user request to Codex, the system detecting an unsafe action, an approval prompt being sent, the user responding, and finally the action being executed or applied.
This file serves as an entry point for organizing and running integration tests that verify the Codex tool functionality works correctly end-to-end.

This file implements a debug admin interface that lets operators monitor and control the network proxy's behavior through HTTP endpoints. It provides read-only status checks (health, current configuration, access patterns, and blocked connections) plus administrative actions to switch network modes and reload settings.

This file manages configuration settings for the network proxy feature, including what ports it listens on, what access modes are enabled, and which domains are allowed or blocked. It reads proxy configuration from settings files and applies security policies—such as restricting external network access by default and preventing unsafe exposure of local system services like Docker sockets.

This file implements an HTTP proxy server that intercepts and controls network requests based on security policies. It handles three types of requests: standard HTTP requests, HTTPS tunneling (CONNECT), and local Unix socket connections—checking each against network policies, blocking rules, and access modes before allowing or denying them. The file also logs blocked requests and strips unnecessary headers before forwarding approved requests upstream.

This file serves as the public interface for the network proxy system, exposing the key components that other parts of the product use to control network traffic and enforce connection policies. It organizes and makes available the configuration options, policy decision-making logic, and runtime controls that allow the product to manage how and where data can be sent over the network.

This file enables the network proxy to track and identify individual code execution attempts by embedding a unique attempt identifier into proxy authentication credentials, allowing the system to correlate network requests back to their originating code run.

This file controls whether Codex is allowed to make network requests to specific hosts and ports. It defines the rules and decision-making logic that determine if a connection should be allowed, blocked, or escalated to ask the user for permission—taking into account factors like the protocol being used, the destination host, and any custom policies configured by administrators.

This file enforces network security policies by validating which domains and IP addresses the product is allowed to connect to. It prevents unsafe connections to private networks (like localhost or internal company servers) and provides pattern matching to allow or block specific domains based on administrator-defined rules.

This file sets up and manages Codex's network proxy service, which intercepts and controls all network traffic from sandboxed code execution. # ChatComposer Summary

The `ChatComposer` is the interactive text input widget for the chat interface. It manages everything the user types, pastes, and attaches before sending a message.

## Core Responsibilities

**Text Editing & Input**
- Handles typing, pasting, and deletion with a rich textarea that supports atomic elements (like image placeholders and slash commands)
- Detects "paste bursts" (rapid input that looks like pasted content from the clipboard) to distinguish them from human typing
- Expands large pastes into placeholder elements to keep the UI responsive, then restores full content on submission

**Attachments**
- Manages local image files attached by the user (with placeholder labels like `[Image #1]`)
- Tracks remote image URLs separately and renders them as non-editable rows above the textarea
- Automatically renumbers local image placeholders when remote images are added or removed

**History Navigation**
- Stores submitted messages in local in-session history and (via async lookup) persistent cross-session history
- Supports shell-style Up/Down arrow navigation to recall previous messages, with cursor positioned at end-of-line for repeated navigation

**Slash Commands & Popups**
- Recognizes `/command` syntax and shows an autocomplete popup
- Allows Tab to complete commands, Enter to dispatch them
- Supports custom prompts with variable expansion (`$USER`, `$1`–`$9`, etc.)
- Also manages `@file` search popups and `$mention` popups for skills and connectors

**Submission Modes**
- "Steer mode": Enter submits immediately, Tab queues while a task runs
- Standard mode: Enter queues while a task runs, otherwise submits
- Validates commands before submission and reports errors

**State Preservation**
- Tracks pending large-paste payloads so they survive editing and are expanded only on submit
- Records mention bindings so `$skill` references resolve correctly even after history recall
- Clears text on Ctrl+C while preserving it in history for undo-like recall

## Key User-Facing Behavior

- **Paste detection**: Small pastes insert directly; large pastes use placeholders to avoid lag
- **IME-friendly**: Non-ASCII input (from input methods) is never held, avoiding dropped character feeling
- **Image attachment**: Users can paste image file paths and they're automatically detected and attached
- **Shell-like history**: Repeated Up/Down keeps navigating backwards/forwards without stopping at interior cursor positions
- **Read-only mode**: Can be temporarily disabled (e.g. during setup) with a placeholder message shown instead

# ChatWidget File Summary

This file implements the main chat interface for the OpenAI Codex TUI. It manages the conversation viewport, command input, history display, and all user interactions while streaming and executing AI responses.

**Key Responsibilities:**

- **Conversation State**: Maintains message history, active message being streamed, and transcript rendering for both the main viewport and overlay (Ctrl+T)
- **Event Processing**: Consumes protocol events from codex-core (agent messages, command execution, approvals, errors) and updates UI state accordingly
- **User Input**: Routes keyboard commands, slash commands, and composed messages to codex-core; handles special cases like image attachments and mention resolution
- **Agent Feedback**: Displays real-time command output, execution status, MCP server startup progress, and approval requests as work happens
- **Configuration & Modes**: Manages runtime model/effort selection, collaboration modes (Plan/Default/etc.), approval policies, and sandbox settings; persists user choices
- **UI Overlays**: Opens popups for model selection, permissions, status line setup, experimental features, and feedback submission
- **Session Lifecycle**: Handles session creation, resuming existing conversations, thread naming, forking, and token/rate-limit tracking
- **Accessibility**: Provides status-line display with git branch, token usage, context remaining, and custom items; schedules async lookups when needed

The widget acts as a bridge between the low-level chat stream and the terminal UI, translating agent work into visible transcript cells and fielding user intent back to the agent engine.

This file manages the connection between the chat UI and the backend AI engine. It sets up communication channels that allow the UI to send user actions (like typing or commands) to the backend and receive events (like AI responses or status updates) back to display in the chat window.

This file manages a queue of interrupts—events like approval requests, user input prompts, and command execution notifications—that need to be shown to the user in the chat interface. It buffers these events so they can be processed and displayed in the correct order rather than being handled immediately and out of sequence.
This file defines a header component for chat sessions that displays which AI model is currently being used, and allows the model name to be updated when the user switches between different models.

This file manages the skills feature in the chat interface, allowing users to view, enable, and disable available skills. It handles parsing when users mention skills in their input (by typing `$skillname`), and it matches those mentions to the actual skills and apps available in the system to provide intelligent suggestions and context.

# ChatWidget Test Suite

This file contains comprehensive integration tests for the **ChatWidget**—the central UI component that adapts incoming protocol events (from the Codex agent backend) into a terminal UI display and handles user input.

## Purpose

The tests verify that ChatWidget correctly:
- **Handles agent events** (messages, command execution, approvals, reasoning, plans, etc.) and renders them in the chat history
- **Processes user input** (text submission, keyboard shortcuts, image attachments, slash commands) and translates it into operations sent back to the backend
- **Manages UI state** (task running/idle, modal popups, composer text, status indicators, rate limit warnings, etc.)
- **Preserves user data** (text elements, local/remote images, mention bindings, queued messages) through lifecycle events like interrupts and reconnects
- **Renders correctly** at various terminal sizes and UI configurations using snapshot tests

## Key Test Categories

**Message & History Flow**: Tests for replayed messages, multi-turn conversations, image attachments, text elements, and proper history cell rendering.

**Execution & Approval**: Tests for command execution display, approval modals (exec/patch), decision history, unified exec processes, and background terminal management.

**User Input & Submission**: Tests for message submission with images/elements, keyboard shortcuts (Enter/Shift+Enter), Alt+Up history recall, Ctrl+C clearing, and Tab queuing.

**Status & Indicators**: Tests for working status display, context window percentage, token counting, stream reconnection, and runtime metrics.

**Rate Limiting & Prompts**: Tests for rate limit warnings, model switch suggestions, and plan implementation popups.

**Collaboration Modes & Features**: Tests for mode switching (/plan, /collab), personality selection, reasoning effort, and feature gates.

**Review Mode**: Tests for review request handling, commit picker, custom prompts, and branch selection.

**Modals & Popups**: Tests for approval selection, model/reasoning pickers, experimental features, feedback submission, and app connectors.

**Edge Cases**: Tests for interrupted turns, small terminal heights, disabled commands, constrained configs, and invalid status line items.

The tests use snapshot assertions to detect unintended layout or message changes; many render to a VT100 terminal backend to capture the exact visual output.

It saves the current state of the display—whether the overlay menu is active and where the cursor is positioned—so that when the user resumes, the interface can restore itself properly without losing context or visual alignment.

This file defines the standard spacing and alignment measurements used throughout the terminal interface, ensuring that visual elements like the chat composer, status messages, and user history all line up consistently on the screen.

This file determines how to update Codex when the user runs an update check, by detecting which package manager installed the tool (npm, Bun, or Homebrew) and generating the appropriate update command for that manager.

This file manages the update notification dialog that appears when a newer version of Codex is available. It displays the current and latest version numbers, provides release notes, and lets users choose to update immediately, skip the update, or dismiss future reminders about this version.

This file manages software update notifications for Codex. It checks whether a newer version is available (by querying GitHub releases or Homebrew), caches that information to avoid repeated network requests, and provides the UI with version details to display an upgrade banner—while respecting users who have dismissed the notification for a particular version.
This file stores the version number of the Codex command-line tool, automatically pulled from the build configuration so it stays in sync whenever a new release is created.

This file handles text wrapping for the terminal UI, breaking long lines of text into multiple shorter lines that fit within a specified width while preserving styling (like colors) and supporting custom indentation for the first line versus subsequent lines. It's used whenever Codex needs to display text in the terminal that's longer than the available space.

This file serves as the test entry point for the terminal user interface, organizing and running all integration tests that verify the UI works correctly end-to-end—including tests for terminal rendering and the command-line tool itself.

This file organizes a collection of integration tests that verify the terminal user interface works correctly, including tests for startup stability, status display, terminal history, and live commit functionality.

This file tests that the Codex CLI handles configuration errors gracefully without crashing when it starts up. Specifically, it verifies that if the rules configuration is malformed, the application exits with an error message rather than panicking, ensuring a better user experience when users have invalid configuration files.

This file tests that the status display widget properly removes invisible formatting codes (like color instructions) from text before showing it to users, ensuring the display buffer stays clean and doesn't accidentally show raw control characters.

This file contains automated tests that verify the text history display feature works correctly in different scenarios. It tests that text renders properly when inserted into the terminal view, including edge cases like long lines that need to wrap, emoji and international characters, colored text, word boundaries, and cursor positioning.

This file tests how the product handles text overflow when displaying live content (like streaming code or command output). It verifies that when new text is continuously added to a display area, older lines are properly saved to history while recent lines remain visible and ready for updates.

This file is a test utility that exposes a mock terminal backend for testing the user interface without needing actual terminal hardware—allowing tests to verify that the product displays information correctly on screen.

This file provides a utility for handling file paths in a consistent, normalized way throughout the application. It ensures that paths are always converted to absolute paths (from the root of the filesystem) and handles user-friendly shortcuts like `~` for home directories, so the rest of the codebase can work with paths reliably without worrying about whether they're relative or absolute.

This file defines preset security and permission configurations that users can select to control what Codex is allowed to do—such as whether it can edit files, run commands, or access the internet, and when it needs to ask for approval before taking actions. The three built-in presets (Read Only, Default, and Full Access) provide quick setup options for different trust levels, and this code makes them available to both the command-line interface and other parts of the system.

This file provides a memory cache that stores frequently-accessed data to speed up repeated lookups. It automatically discards the least-recently-used items when the cache gets full, and includes a utility function to generate fingerprints of file contents for cache keys, which helps avoid stale data from outdated file paths.

This file helps the Codex product locate built binaries and test resources regardless of whether the code is being built and tested with Cargo (the standard Rust tool) or Bazel (an alternative build system). It provides a compatibility layer so tests can find the files they need without having to know which build system is in use.

This file defines the approval mode options users can select when running Codex from the command line, controlling when the system asks for permission before executing suggested commands—ranging from only trusting safe commands, to asking on-demand, to never asking at all.

This file enables users to override configuration settings from the command line using the `-c key=value` syntax (e.g., `-c model="o3"`). It parses these overrides and applies them to the tool's configuration, supporting nested settings via dotted paths like `foo.bar.baz` and automatically converting values to the correct type.

This file formats environment variables for display in the command-line interface while hiding their actual values for security—showing variable names with masked values (like `API_KEY=*****`) instead of exposing secrets in logs or output.

This file exposes command-line argument handling utilities that allow users to configure Codex's behavior through terminal commands—such as approval mode, sandbox mode, and configuration overrides—by bundling together related argument parsing logic.

This file defines the command-line options for the `--sandbox` flag, which lets users control how much access Codex has to their system—read-only, workspace write, or full access. It translates the user's choice from the command line into the internal system that enforces these security levels.

This file provides a utility to display how long operations take in a human-friendly format—converting raw time measurements into strings like "250ms", "1.50s", or "1m 15s" depending on the duration. It's used throughout the product wherever elapsed time needs to be shown to users, such as in logs, progress indicators, or performance metrics.

This file provides fuzzy text matching for search and filtering features—it finds how well a user's search term matches items in a list (like searching for files or commands). It returns both which characters matched and a quality score, with special handling for case-insensitive matching and international characters to ensure accurate text highlighting.

This file enables Codex to apply code changes (patches) to a Git repository safely and report what happened. It wraps Git's built-in patch-application tool, allowing callers to test changes before committing them (via a "preflight" dry-run mode) and provides detailed feedback on which files were successfully modified, which were skipped due to conflicts, and which had other issues.

This file helps Codex understand the relationship between different branches in a Git repository by finding their common ancestor commit. It's used internally to intelligently choose which branch version to compare against when analyzing code changes, particularly when a remote branch is ahead of the local one.

This file defines error messages that the system displays when something goes wrong while managing Git repositories—such as when a Git command fails, a path is invalid, or a file can't be read. It ensures users get clear, specific feedback about what went wrong and why.

This file implements Codex's "ghost commit" feature, which creates hidden snapshots of your repository's current state and lets you undo changes by restoring from those snapshots. It captures both tracked and untracked files, intelligently excludes large files and common dependency folders to keep snapshots lightweight, and supports restoring your working directory to any previous snapshot while preserving files that existed before the snapshot was made.

This file provides Git operations utilities for Codex, particularly managing "ghost commits" — temporary snapshots of a repository's state that can be created and restored. It exposes functions for applying patches, managing branches, creating snapshots of uncommitted work, and handling platform-specific operations like creating symlinks, helping the system preserve and recover code states during editing sessions.

This file handles all the basic Git operations that the system needs to perform, such as verifying a folder is a Git repository, finding the current commit, locating the repository root directory, and running Git commands in general. It acts as a bridge between the product and Git, translating Git operations into reliable results and catching errors with helpful context.

This file handles creating symbolic links (shortcuts) in a way that works on both Mac/Linux and Windows computers, since the two operating systems handle symlinks differently under the hood. It's part of the git utilities that support Codex's file system operations across different platforms.

This file locates where Codex stores its configuration and settings files on a user's computer, checking the `CODEX_HOME` environment variable first (if set) and falling back to a `.codex` folder in the user's home directory if not. It validates that any custom location actually exists and is a proper directory before allowing the system to use it.

This file defines error messages that appear when the system encounters problems working with images—such as failing to open an image file, failing to read its contents, or failing to save it in a particular format. It helps the product communicate clearly to users when image operations go wrong.

This file handles preparing images for upload to the AI model by loading them from disk, automatically shrinking oversized images to fit within 2048×768 pixels, and converting them to standard web formats (PNG or JPEG). It caches processed images in memory to avoid re-processing the same file multiple times.

This file converts configuration data from JSON format into TOML format, allowing the system to accept configuration input in JSON and transform it into the TOML format used internally for settings and configuration files.

This file provides shared utilities for working with open-source AI model providers (like LM Studio and Ollama), allowing the product to look up default models and verify that a provider is properly set up and ready to use.

This file provides the public interface for spawning and managing processes in different modes—either in a traditional interactive terminal environment or using simple pipes for non-interactive execution. It exposes helper functions and data structures that other parts of the codebase use to launch subprocesses and communicate with them.

This file manages running external programs (like shell commands) as separate processes and lets the Codex system communicate with them. It handles sending input to the program, capturing its output, and detecting when the program finishes—all without using a terminal interface.

This file defines the core control interface for running interactive terminal sessions or piped processes. It provides a `ProcessHandle` that lets other parts of the system send input to a running process, receive its output in real-time, check if it has exited, and terminate it when needed—essentially acting as the bridge between Codex and any subprocess it spawns.

This file provides utilities to safely clean up spawned commands and their child processes across different operating systems. It handles critical details like isolating commands from the terminal, ensuring child processes don't outlive their parent, and reliably terminating entire process families (not just individual processes) when a command needs to be stopped.

This file enables Codex to run terminal commands (like shell scripts or executables) in isolated virtual terminal environments and communicate with them. It handles starting the process, capturing its output in real-time, accepting input from the user, and detecting when the process exits—working across both Windows and Unix-like systems.

This file contains automated tests that verify the behavior of Codex's process execution system. It tests two key ways of running external programs—one using a traditional pipe-based approach and another using a pseudo-terminal (PTY) that mimics interactive terminal behavior—ensuring they both work correctly, produce output as expected, handle edge cases like stderr draining, and cleanly shut down when requested.

This file enables Codex to create and manage terminal windows on Windows by implementing the underlying communication layer between the code editor and a Windows command prompt or PowerShell session. It handles setting up the virtual terminal, resizing it when needed, and routing input/output between the editor and the running terminal process.

This file enables Codex to run and manage terminal processes on Windows by providing the low-level Windows-specific logic for spawning shells, monitoring their status, and terminating them when needed. It acts as a bridge between the general terminal management system and Windows' native process control APIs.
This file handles the low-level Windows system setup needed to connect a terminal session to a pseudo-console, enabling Codex to create and manage terminal windows on Windows machines.

This file enables Codex to run terminal commands on Windows by creating and managing pseudo-consoles—virtual terminal environments that let the system execute programs as if they were running in a real terminal. It handles setting up these virtual terminals, resizing them when needed, and launching programs within them with proper environment variables and working directories.

This file implements a readiness flag — a synchronization mechanism that allows the system to signal when a service is ready to handle requests. It uses a token-based authorization system where callers must obtain a subscription token before they can mark the flag as ready, and other parts of the system can wait until the flag transitions to ready. This is useful for coordinating service startup or initialization sequences.

This file ensures that the codebase has a working cryptography provider for secure connections. It sets up the cryptography system once when the application starts so that SSL/TLS connections work properly throughout the product.

This file generates a human-readable summary of the system's configuration settings, displaying key information like the working directory, AI model being used, security approval requirements, and sandbox restrictions—with additional reasoning-related settings shown when applicable for certain model types.

This file serves as a public entry point that exposes two key utilities: one for generating summaries of configuration settings and another for summarizing sandbox security policies. It acts as a bridge between the internal implementation details and the rest of the codebase.

This file generates human-readable descriptions of sandbox security settings for code execution. It translates technical security policies (like which folders a code execution environment can access and whether it has internet connectivity) into short, clear labels that can be shown in the UI or logs.

This file automatically detects and hides sensitive information like API keys, passwords, and authentication tokens from text before it's logged or displayed, protecting users' credentials from accidental exposure.

This file provides a no-op fallback implementation for preventing the system from sleeping, used when the platform doesn't have native sleep-prevention support or as a placeholder for testing purposes.

This file prevents the computer from going to sleep while Codex is actively processing a request. On macOS, it uses the system's native power management APIs to keep the machine awake; on other systems, it's a no-op placeholder.

This file prevents macOS from going to sleep while Codex is actively running a task. It communicates with macOS's power management system to request that the computer stay awake, and releases that request when the task is complete.

This file provides utility functions for safely handling text in Codex, particularly for truncating strings to fit size limits and cleaning up metric labels. It ensures that when text needs to be shortened or used as system metadata, it's done correctly without breaking special characters and only allows valid characters in metrics tracking.
This file configures the Windows installer for Codex Sandbox by attaching a manifest file that specifies system requirements and permissions needed when the application runs on Windows.

This file manages Windows file and folder permissions for the Codex sandbox environment. It provides functions to read, check, and modify access control rules—allowing the system to grant or deny read, write, and execute permissions to specific users or processes on files and directories, and to ensure sandboxed code has the access it needs while being properly restricted.

This file determines which folders and files a sandboxed code execution environment is allowed to read and write to. It enforces security policies by identifying writable workspace directories while explicitly blocking access to Git metadata (`.git` folders), and optionally includes temporary directories based on the configured security level.

This file scans the file system to identify folders that anyone on the system can write to, then locks down those folders to prevent the sandboxed code from accessing them. It runs a quick security audit when the sandbox starts up, checks commonly-used directories (temp folders, the current working directory, system paths), and if it finds any world-writable locations, it applies restrictions so the sandboxed process can't exploit them.

This file is an entry point that ensures the command runner tool only runs on Windows systems; it routes execution to the Windows-specific implementation and prevents the tool from launching on other operating systems.

This file serves as the entry point for a Windows sandbox setup tool, routing execution to Windows-specific code when run on Windows or halting with an error message if someone tries to run it on other operating systems.

This file manages security identifiers (SIDs) that control sandbox permissions in the Windows sandbox environment. It generates unique identifiers for different workspaces and directories, stores them persistently, and retrieves them to ensure each workspace is properly isolated from others during code execution.

This file is the main entry point for securely running user code on Windows with restricted permissions. It reads execution instructions from a request file, sets up a sandboxed environment with limited access based on a security policy, launches the process with restricted credentials, and monitors it until completion or timeout.

This file manages directory shortcuts (junctions) on Windows that allow the Codex sandbox to access the user's working directory. It creates these shortcuts in a hidden `.codex` folder, reusing existing ones when possible to keep performance fast, and handles any errors that occur during creation.

This file provides encryption and decryption capabilities for sensitive data on Windows machines, allowing the system to securely store and retrieve information at the machine level so that both regular and elevated processes can access it.

This file implements the core sandboxing execution engine for Windows, enabling Codex to safely run user commands in an isolated environment with restricted permissions. It launches a separate sandbox process under a restricted user account, enforces access policies (read-only, workspace-write, or network restrictions), and captures the command's output and exit code while respecting timeouts.

This file configures environment variables for code execution in a sandboxed Windows environment. It handles tasks like disabling network access, ensuring commands run non-interactively, and blocking dangerous tools—all to make the sandbox safer and more predictable when running untrusted code.

This file sets up Windows firewall rules to block outbound network traffic from sandboxed code execution environments, preventing them from making external network connections when operating offline. It creates or updates a persistent firewall rule tied to a specific user account, verifies the rule was applied correctly, and logs all configuration changes.

This file hides temporary user accounts and their profile directories from appearing in Windows login screens and file explorers. It works by marking user profile folders as hidden and updating Windows system settings to prevent newly created sandbox users from being visible to the end user.

This file manages the identity and credentials needed to run code inside the Windows sandbox. It checks whether the sandbox has been properly set up, retrieves or creates the sandbox user account and its encrypted password, and returns those credentials so the system can log in and execute commands in the isolated environment.

This file is the main entry point for Windows sandboxing functionality that isolates and controls code execution in a restricted environment. It organizes specialized security modules (like access control, permissions, encryption, and process management), exposes their key features to the rest of the product, and provides the core capability to run commands in a sandboxed process with configurable restrictions on file access, network access, and user permissions—while also providing fallback stubs for non-Windows systems.

This file handles logging for the Windows Sandbox tool, recording what commands were run, whether they succeeded or failed, and any debug information. It writes timestamped log entries to a `sandbox.log` file, truncating long commands to keep logs readable while being careful not to corrupt text with special characters.

This file standardizes file paths so that different ways of writing the same path (like using backslashes vs. forward slashes, or different capitalization) are treated as identical. This ensures the product reliably recognizes when two file references actually point to the same location, which is important for things like caching or deduplication.

This file controls what security policies can be applied to the sandboxed environment where code executes. It accepts policy names or configurations as input, validates them to block dangerous access levels, and returns the approved security policy—ensuring that generated code can only run with safe, restricted permissions.

This file handles launching new processes within a Windows sandbox environment. It prepares the necessary configuration (environment variables, command-line arguments, working directory, and input/output streams) and then starts a process with a restricted security token, which is essential for the sandbox's security isolation.

This file manages exclusive access to reading file permissions in the Windows sandbox environment, ensuring that only one process can perform this operation at a time. It provides a way to check if another process is already reading permissions and to safely claim that lock for the current process.

This file sets up and manages Windows user accounts needed to run code in isolated sandboxes. It creates two special sandbox users (one for offline work, one for online work), assigns them random passwords, stores those credentials securely, and organizes them into a dedicated Windows user group for access control.

This file defines how the sandbox setup process reports and tracks errors. It catalogs all the ways the sandbox initialization can fail (from file creation to firewall configuration), and provides tools to record these failures, read them back, and safely report them in usage metrics without exposing sensitive user information like file paths or usernames.

This file sets up the Windows sandbox environment for Codex by configuring file permissions, user accounts, and firewall rules. It receives configuration details (which folders to read/write, which users to create, etc.), then applies the appropriate Windows security settings to enforce those restrictions on sandboxed code execution.

This file orchestrates the setup and initialization of Windows sandbox environments for Codex. It manages launching a privileged helper process that configures sandboxed user accounts, file system access permissions (read and write roots), and sandbox state markers—either with elevated privileges when needed or as a simple refresh of existing sandbox settings.

This file manages Windows security tokens for sandboxed processes, creating restricted user accounts with limited permissions so that code running in the sandbox cannot access sensitive system resources or escape the sandbox. It provides utilities to generate tokens with specific capability restrictions, configure file access permissions, and enable only the minimal privileges needed for sandboxed operations like creating inter-process communication pipes.

This file provides utility functions that help the codebase interact with Windows systems, particularly for handling text encoding, command-line argument formatting, and decoding system error messages. It ensures that commands and data are properly formatted according to Windows conventions so they can be safely passed to Windows system calls.

This file manages security permissions for Codex workspace directories in Windows sandboxed environments. Specifically, it checks whether a command is running from a workspace root and prevents write access to a hidden `.codex` folder to protect Codex's internal files from being modified.
